blocks.text: Lecture 1: Logistic Regression
Andrei A Simion
Columbia University
September 1, 2025
Me: Andrei Simion
My Job: Applied Scientist at Goldman Sachs working on Gen AI Applications
Worked in Tech/Finance for a few years
PhD IEOR/CS 2015 in Convex (!) Optimization for NLP Mike Collins and Cliff Stein
Class Policy: Ask stupid questions, please - a stupid question is 100% better than hiding because you are afraid you'll look dumb
No one knows everything
Please use Courseworks or email me / TAs if you have questions
Use Ed! Please!
Schedule of Topics
Weeks 1 - 6: Logistic Regression, Back Propagation, Optimization, Word2Vec, RNN / GNN / CNN
Week 7 7/14/25: Midterm
Weeks 8 - 12: Transformers, BERT, GPT, LORA, DPO/RHLF
Week 13: Review, catch up, more applications of what we looked at (Information Retrieval, Ads, Scaling Laws)
Final will be held during the University's scheduled period
Grades
How will you get a grade?
Mostly via HW and Midterm / Final
6 or so assignments, you need to do 6 but it is easy to get full extra credit
If you do more than 6, you get extra credit (each HW is worth 10 points) but max score before exams is 70
Each assignment is worth the same
HW: 60 Midterm: 20 Final: 20
You need to take the Midterm and the Final regardless
Midterm and Final might be above 20 points; in which case these problems will be extra credit
HW is due about 2 weeks after it is assigned
The course objective: know enough NLP / ML from this class so you can impress on a job interview
If you want: you can write a survey paper and implement a model and/or try to extend some result and submit it: EMNLP / ACL / NAACL
I can help with this
5 pages is enough for a 'short' paper (can also be for a workshop)
Grades: To get A+, you need to do well on exams and HW
Score = min(sum(HW + Paper), 70) + Midterm + Final
Total points example (lots of extra HW but poor on exams): Score = (min(100, 70) + 5 + 5) = 80 = B-
Total points example (minimal HW but good on exams):

blocks.section_title: Grades: To get A+, you need to do well on exams and HW

blocks.text: If you get a total Score ∼ 90 you get A-
If you get a total Score ∼ 100 you get A+ (you did well on exams and did the minimal HW)
If you get a total Score ∼ 93 you get A
If you get a total Score ∼ 82 you get B-
If you get a Score ∼ 78 you get C+
If you get a Score ∼ 62 you get D-
If you get a Score < 60 you get F
Might rescale this is needed to get some bell curve depending on the distribution; but I'll only rescale if it can help people
Office Hours, Ed, Zoom
My Office Hours (Tentative):
After class (in person)
Will try for Wednesday before class also - will announce on Ed
TAs: We have a few and I'm unsure if they will have office hours - need to discuss with them
I'll try to go about 1.5 hours - 2 hours each week, not 2.5
Use Ed!!
You can attend class via Zoom or in person, I don't take attendance
What background / compute do I need?
Do I need to know lots of Deep Learning / ML for this class?
Probably not, but you need to know Math + Python (Conda / NumPy) or you can pick up Python
I'll try to add in things as we go so there a little explanation for different things or a reference you can use to fill in a knowledge gap
For this class, one goal I have is to go over details of some famous (NLP) papers and not just textbook material
I.e. What is Word2Vec?
I.e. How does BERT work when you apply it to different tasks? (Let's actually look at the internals) GPT?
Colab would be great so you have GPU access (especially if you want to do a project), but I'll try to make the work in such a way that you don't need it
HW is meant for you to understand the basics, not do heavy compute experiments

Logistic Regression Assumptions
We assume that the probability y depends on x via a linear transformation
So y ∼ θ ⊺ x but we need a probability for y , since y is 1/0 and this probability sufficiently explains y
blocks.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression Assumptions We assume that the probability y depends on x via a linear transformation
blocks.nearby_text_after: To get a probability, we use p ( y = 1 | x; θ ) = g ( θ ⊺ x ) where g is some function that maps R → [0, 1] θ ⊺ x can be anything, and so the domain of g is R The range of g must be [0, 1] since g ( θ ⊺ x ) is a probability

blocks.text: Logistic Regression with σ
σ ( z ) = 1 1+ e -z has some nice properties, for example
blocks.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression with σ
blocks.nearby_text_after: Note also that σ is monotonic in z This implies, for example that we have p ( y = 1 | x; θ ) = σ ( θ ⊺ x ) increasing in each θ j if all others are fixed Note that this model is easy to interpret: if θ j > 0 this means that as x j gets larger p ( y = 1 | x; θ ) gets larger

blocks.text: One-Hot representation: review this more if you need it
Features f that are categorical can be expressed as one-hot vectors: if f takes on 4 values f is a vector of 1/0 values and exactly one of these values is 1
Similarly, the response for logistic regression can be written as a one-hot vector: 0 can map to (1, 0) and 1 can map to (0, 1)
One-hot representations are important since they allow us to represent a categorical feature or response as a succinct vector
But they have a problem
Assume we have a feature f that has 4 categories and categories are very much related (say'Math' = 0, 'Physics' = 1, 'Painting' = 2, 'Writing' = 3)
If we have two instances f (1) = 'Math' and f (2) = 'Physics' which are have one-hot representations (1, 0, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 0, 0) then ( f (1) ) ⊺ f (2) = 0
blocks.nearby_text_before: One-hot representations are important since they allow us to represent a categorical feature or response as a succinct vector But they have a problem Assume we have a feature f that has 4 categories and categories are very much related (say'Math' = 0, 'Physics' = 1, 'Painting' = 2, 'Writing' = 3)
blocks.nearby_text_after: But, this might not make sense: ideally the inner product captures some 'similarity' between the two vectors since Math and Physics are related

blocks.text: How do we get the gradients with data? Do we need them analytically always?
No! In PyTorch / Tensorflow there are numerical methods that give us the gradients automatically; we'll see some examples as we go
But it is good to know what the gradients are so we understand what is going on

blocks.text: Logistic Regression Computational Graph: What is the flow of data?

blocks.section_title: Logistic Regression Computational Graph: What is the flow of data?
blocks.visual_description: The inputs x, α, and β feed into a linear combination z = βx + α, which is then passed through the sigmoid function a = σ(z). The activated output a and label y determine the logistic loss ℓ = y log a + (1 - y) log(1 - a), showing the forward computation used in logistic regression.
blocks.figure_focus: Logistic regression forward computation and loss

blocks.text: Logistic Regression Computational Graph
Note: this is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
The parameters and data are the leaves: they have no 'in' arcs
There are no cycles
Generally, this will be how graphs look like and these graphs help us reason about what the chain rule gives so we can get gradients

Another Example: Overfitting and the DAG for this case
When we fit a model to the training data, we usually don't just care about Training performance
We also care about performance on another separate Test set
Typically, to control this, we add a regularization term R ( θ ) ≥ 0 which is positive and which controls model complexity
blocks.nearby_text_before: Another Example: Overfitting and the DAG for this case When we fit a model to the training data, we usually don't just care about Training performance We also care about performance on another separate Test set
blocks.nearby_text_after: The 2-norm is R ( θ ) = ∑ d j =1 θ 2 j and typically this makes θ j small but = 0 The 1-norm is R ( θ ) = ∑ d j =1 | θ j | and typically this makes θ sparse so that very few θ j are nonzero Given the regularization, we fix λ > 0 and then focus on max θ ℓ ( θ ) -λ R ( θ )

Regularized Logistic Regression: Computational Graph

blocks.visual_description: Inputs α, β, x, y, and λ feed a logistic regression computation graph. The hidden computations show z = βx + α, a = σ(z), the log-likelihood ℓ = y log a + (1 - y) log(1 - a), a regularization term R = (α² + β²), and the final regularized objective ℓλ = ℓ - λR.

blocks.formula_latex: \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \alpha} = \left(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \ell}\right)\left(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial a}\right)\left(\frac{\partial a}{\partial z}\right)\left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial \alpha}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial R}\right)\left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial \alpha}\right)
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula writes the derivative of \(\ell\) with respect to \(\alpha\) as a sum of contributions along two visible paths. One path passes through \(z\), \(a\), and \(\ell\); the other passes through \(R\).

AUC: A Review
What makes a model good at classification for 1/0 response?
Logistic Regression gives us two types of probabilities, p ( y = 1) and p ( y = 0)
blocks.nearby_text_before: What makes a model good at classification for 1/0 response?
blocks.nearby_text_after: Imagine you have a imbalanced data, where 95% of the data is y = 0 and y = 1 is only 5% of the data Accuracy is not good here, since trivial classifiers give good results A random classifier (predicting y = 1 with 5%) would give an accuracy of approximately ∼ 90%
blocks.text_raw: Logistic Regression gives us two types of probabilities, p ( y = 1) and p ( y = 0)
blocks.text_cleaned: Logistic Regression gives us two types of probabilities, p(y = 1) and p(y = 0)

blocks.text: Imagine you have a imbalanced data, where 95% of the data is y = 0 and y = 1 is only 5% of the data
Accuracy is not good here, since trivial classifiers give good results
A random classifier (predicting y = 1 with 5%) would give an accuracy of approximately ∼ 90%
A deterministic classifier that always predicts 0 would give an accuracy of 95% too
Other metrics, like Recall and Precision came about to address this; an intuitive one you should know is AUC
There are a few ways to understand AUC (i.e. with ROC graphics) but this one you might not know
What should a good model do?
blocks.nearby_text_before: There are a few ways to understand AUC (i.e. with ROC graphics) but this one you might not know
blocks.nearby_text_after: Generally, we would hope it gives a higher score p ( y = 1) on average to response cases with y = 1 (positives) than with y = 0 (negatives) This is the idea of the AUC; let P and N be the positive and negative samples we have in a dataset and g be our model, which returns p ( y = 1) A good model, intuitively, gives class 1 scores close to 1 for y ∈ P and close to zero for y ∈ N

blocks.text: We then have
AUC = ∑ i ∈P ∑ j ∈N 1 {g (x (i)) > g (x (j))} |N| |P|

blocks.formula_explanation: The formula computes AUC by averaging, over positive examples \(i\in P\), the fraction of negative examples \(j\in N\) whose score is ranked below the positive one. It uses an indicator that is 1 exactly when \(g(x^{(i)})>g(x^{(j)})\).

blocks.text: AUC Intuition
AUC = ∑ i ∈P ∑ j ∈N 1 {g (x (i)) > g (x (j))} |N| |P|
blocks.nearby_text_after: Put another way A good model should score points in P higher than it does points in N The AUC as expressed above is an average, where we consider the average number of times a particular positive point is higher than all negative ones
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula computes AUC by averaging, over positive examples, the fraction of negative examples that are scored lower. The indicator counts when a positive score exceeds a negative score, and the result is normalized by the sizes of P and N.

blocks.text: Put another way:
A good model should score points in P higher than it does points in N
The AUC as expressed above is an average, where we consider the average number of times a particular positive point is higher than all negative ones
Then, we average over all positive points
What is a bad AUC?
If you consider the above, you can reason that a 50% value is bad, as it would be the AUC of a random classifier
Why don't we just optimize AUC?
If we care about AUC, why don't we just optimize it directly?
AUC = ∑ i ∈P ∑ j ∈N 1 {g (x (i)) > g (x (j))} |N| |P|
blocks.nearby_text_before: Why don't we just optimize AUC? If we care about AUC, why don't we just optimize it directly?
blocks.nearby_text_after: The above does not have a gradient that's easy to compute Loosely, a higher Log-Likelihood leads to a higher AUC Crucially, the Log-Likelihood is much easier to optimize
blocks.section_title: Why don't we just optimize AUC?
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula computes AUC by averaging, over positive examples, the fraction of negative examples ranked below each positive one. The indicator counts pairwise score comparisons where a positive score exceeds a negative score.

blocks.text: The above does not have a gradient that's easy to compute
Loosely, a higher Log-Likelihood leads to a higher AUC
Crucially, the Log-Likelihood is much easier to optimize

What about Multiclass AUC?
Generally, for a model predicting K classes, we get a vector of probabilities p ( i ) = ( p ( i ) 1 , p ( i ) 2 , ..., p ( i ) K ) for each x ( i )

How do we get an AUC in this case?
Fix a class k and for each point i take just p ( i ) k
Label each y ( i ) as 1/0 where 1 means the true class is k and 0 is anything else
Compute a AUC k which is like the AUC in 2 dimensions for probability p ( i ) k and the vector y ( i )

If the distribution is flat, its hard to say what K is ideal

blocks.visual_description: The graphic contrasts a flat token probability distribution with a peaked token probability distribution to illustrate why a fixed Top-K value can be problematic. In the flat case, several tokens have similar probabilities, while in the peaked case one token dominates and the rest are much smaller, suggesting that the appropriate number of sampled candidates should vary with distribution shape.
blocks.figure_focus: Top-K sampling fails under flat vs peaked distributions

blocks.visual_description: The figure compares text generation outputs from several decoding methods, showing how some methods continue a whale-stranding sentence with repetitive or incoherent completions while others produce more varied continuations. It contrasts WebText, beam search, pure sampling, temperature-controlled sampling, top-k sampling, top-k with temperature, and nucleus sampling, with highlighted words indicating repetition or incoherence. The caption states that maximization and top-k truncation tend to cause copious repetition, while sampling with and without temperature tends to cause incoherence, and that nucleus sampling largely avoids both issues.
blocks.figure_focus: Text generation decoding methods and repetition vs incoherence

Beam Search, Low temperature, small K in TopK )

blocks.text: GPT3 - Hidden Learning
The authors make the argument that while you are predicting the next word, you actually learn In-Context Learning
within a single sequence.

blocks.nearby_text_before: within a single sequence.
blocks.visual_description: A language model meta-learning mechanism is shown with an outer loop labeled learning via SGD during unsupervised pre-training and an inner loop labeled in-context learning. Three example sequences illustrate repeated sub-tasks within a single sequence, including arithmetic, misspelling correction, and translation pairs, indicating how broad skills learned in pre-training support rapid task adaptation at inference time.
blocks.figure_focus: Language model meta-learning with in-context learning

blocks.text: GPT3 - Learning
Traditional fine-tuning (not used for GPT-3)
The goal of the GPT3 paper was to show that a large model can do well without gradient updates but better prompting task descripe
In addition to the task description, the model sees a few examples of the task. No gradient updates are performed.
Translate English to French:
sea otter » loutre de ner pepperaint » menthe polvrée

blocks.nearby_text_before: In addition to the task description, the model sees a few examples of the task. No gradient updates are performed. Translate English to French: sea otter » loutre de ner pepperaint » menthe polvrée
blocks.visual_description: The graphic compares three in-context learning settings—zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot—showing that the model receives a task description alone or with example input-output pairs, with no gradient updates. It contrasts this with traditional fine-tuning, where repeated gradient updates are applied to many example tasks before producing a prompt answer.
blocks.figure_focus: In-context learning vs. traditional fine-tuning

Goal: From scratch, train a smaller BERT and have it get similar performance
Specifically, get the same (or very close) performance to BERT but with fewer parameters
3 Main Optimizations

Replace NSP with Sentence Order Prediction (SOP)
Share parameters between Encoder transformer blocks

blocks.visual_description: A comparison table reports performance of three loss settings for embedding-related tasks and downstream benchmarks. It shows that no extra loss, NSP, and SOP yield different scores on intrinsic tasks (MLM, NSP, SOP) and downstream tasks (SQuAD1.1, SQuAD2.0, MNLI, SST-2, RACE), with SOP giving the best overall average. The values suggest SOP improves several downstream results more consistently than NSP.

blocks.text: ALBERT: inference time vs BERT
ALBERT-xxlarge has 200 Million parameters
BERT-large has 340 Million parameters
However, ALBERT-xxlarge has a larger structure, so inference is still slower
Authors discuss some other papers which could be used as a guide to speed it up
Big Idea: It's not just all about more parameters!
Vision Transformer (ViT)
After BERT came out, other variants like ALBERT came out
Some dealt with text, but others dealt with other types of data as well
We saw CNNs for image classification and we used them to even do text classification
Can we take the Transformer architectures we've seen for text and use them for image classification (i.e. the reverse)?
Answer: Yes
Vision Transformer
At a high level, this is how a Vision Transformer works ...
Take the Transformer Encoder and use it to somehow ingest a picture and predict an image class
But for a Transform we had tokens, what are the tokens here?
Take a 224 × 224 × 3 picture
Break it up into patches of size 16 × 16 × 3
blocks.nearby_text_before: Take the Transformer Encoder and use it to somehow ingest a picture and predict an image class But for a Transform we had tokens, what are the tokens here? Take a 224 × 224 × 3 picture

blocks.text: Each of these patches are 'tokens' and we need them to be vectors so we map (using a CNN or MLP) each small volume to a vector
For a Transformer, we need to break ties using positional embeddings, so add these
As usual, feed everything through the Transformer blocks and get the final representations
But, we need a prediction to make gradient updates and learn ... Add a CLS token at the start of the token sequence
Map the final CLS token's embedding through a MLP and predict the class of the picture (MNIST, CIFAR-10, etc)
Back propagate!
Given enough data, we get a very powerful classifier
Vision Transformer in Pictures
Vision Transformer (ViT)
Vaswani et al. (2017).

Natural Language Degeneration
GPT2
GPT3
ALBERT + Vision Transformer
Adaptors
LORA
Fine Tuning
Given a transformer, what options do we have to use it for a specific task?
Let's focus on Classification and BERT, where we add a MLP on top of the CLS token
Option1: We can optimize just the 'head' MLP parameters
Using BERT as a feature extractor
This is relatively cheap
We do not, however, get SOTA on tasks
Option 2: We can optimize ALL of the parameters (BERT + MLP)
This is fine-tuning
We can get SOTA
But, the big problem is that this is quite expensive
Is there something in-between?
We saw one example with ULM-Fit: different learning rates were used per layer
Adapter

The idea of an adapter is to insert a few critical layers in a transformer and allow gradients only on parameters from these layers The adapter also contains a skip-connection. During adapter tuning, the green layers are trained on the downstream data, this includes the adapter, the layer normalization parameters, and the
If you pick the architecture right, you can save time on fine tuning
Generally, this will increase the inference cost
Big idea: insert a small MLP, tune the LayerNorm parameters
Let d model be the embedding dimensions, and d adapter be the MLP

blocks.visual_description: The figure explains adapter-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning for Transformers. An adapter module is inserted twice in each Transformer layer, once after multi-head attention and once after the feed-forward block, and it contains a bottleneck structure with down-projection, nonlinearity, and up-projection plus a skip connection. During adapter training, only the adapter and selected normalization/classification parameters are updated while the original model weights remain fixed.
blocks.figure_focus: Transformer adapter module architecture

A line plot compares validation accuracy as adapter initialization magnitude varies, with MNLI staying nearly flat over a wide range and CoLA dropping sharply at the largest initialization scale. Adaptors
LORA
LORA
Both adapters and other methods like prefix-tuning (see references) have some issues
Adapters introduce high inference latency
Prefix-tuning is difficult to optimize, and performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters
Also, reserving a part of the sequence length for adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task; might this lead to degradation in performance?
LORA

LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix W k , set W k = W k + BA where B , A have low rank r << min { d k , d model } and B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel Pretrained Weights WE Raxa
blocks.nearby_text_after: LORA: Low-Rank Adaptation Initialize B to all zeros and A to Gaussian noise so BA = 0 at the start but gradient updates are not trivially 'stuck'

The input x is transformed through the pretrained weights plus the low-rank adaptation to yield h.

blocks.text: Benefits of LORA
Why would LORA be a good idea?
Generalized full fine-tuning - if we select r to to the right choice and have a LORA set B , A for each matrix, we basically have fine tuning
Adapters converge to an MLP
Prefix models cannot handle longer sequences
Benefit of LORA: no additional layers, so no additional inference latency
Another benefit: at test time, we can use W = W + BA , just a new matrix ... We always need to load 1 model
blocks.nearby_text_before: Adapters converge to an MLP Prefix models cannot handle longer sequences Benefit of LORA: no additional layers, so no additional inference latency
blocks.nearby_text_after: Yet another: we can swap in different BA for different tasks, so it's easy to 'adapt' the model For their paper, the authors focus on just the Self Attention parameters like W k or W q; they freeze all the other parameters

blocks.text: Recommendation Problems: What do we need to do?
Given a problem, there are many things to consider
What data do we have?
What labels do we have?
What models do we use?
How do we know what metrics to look at? Offline metrics? Online metrics?
What is the scale? Is it serving fast enough?
Do recommendations need to be fresh upon request or can they be somewhat stale?
What else?
Outline
Recommendation Problems
Metrics
Design Youtube Video Search
Metrics
Let's step back a second ...
Suppose we have a model such that, given some query, gives us the most relevant items ranked from best to worst
How do we know that this is doing well?
Goal: Have a metric such that if we do well we have a value of 1 if we do poorly we have a value of 0
'Well' here means that the results returned are relevant to the user
Offline Metrics: Recall
For a certain set of queries, we might know the items that should be returned
Suppose our system always returns 5 items ranked from best to worst
Suppose that for a query q we have a universe of items I ( q ) which are relevant
For some query, this might be 1 item while for another query this might be 1000 items
Given we made 5 ranked predictions, we can then compute
Recall 5 = Number of relevant items among the top 5 predictions | I (q) |
blocks.nearby_text_before: Suppose that for a query q we have a universe of items I ( q ) which are relevant For some query, this might be 1 item while for another query this might be 1000 items Given we made 5 ranked predictions, we can then compute
blocks.formula_latex: \mathrm{Recall}_5 = \frac{\text{Number of relevant items among the top 5 predictions}}{|I(q)|}
blocks.formula_explanation: This defines recall at 5 as the number of relevant items found in the top 5 predictions divided by the total number of relevant items for query \(q\). It measures how much of the relevant set is captured by the top-ranked results.

blocks.text: Offline Metrics: Recall
What's the idea: we've made 5 predictions, and we want to know of these 5 how many actually are sensible with respect to the universe of all relevant items

blocks.text: Offline Metrics: nDCG
DCG @ k = k ∑ i =1 rel i log 2 (i +1)

As a final metric, recall we have AUC given by
AUC = ∑ i ∈P ∑ j ∈N 1 {g (q, v i) > g (q, v j)} |N| |P|

Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
All the metrics we've discussed above are Offline Metrics: we have a machine learning model and we want to get it to rank things as well as possible
The analysis we do is done in a research / notebook setting, using historical data ... What happens once we deploy?
In that setting, a user types a query and then gets list of results
... How do we tell if the results were good? How do we build a larger system that uses the ranking model we evaluated?
Let's first look at the basic metrics which we would use to evaluate System-level performance
Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
CTR = Number of Clicks Number of Impressions
blocks.nearby_text_before: Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
blocks.nearby_text_after: There are many other ways to also engage ... For example if we present items to buy and the user buys them, we can the conversion rate
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines CTR as the number of clicks divided by the number of impressions. It measures how often an item is clicked when it is shown.

blocks.text: There are many other ways to also engage
... For example if we present items to buy and the user buys them, we can the conversion rate
CVR = Number of Conversions Number of Impressions
blocks.nearby_text_before: Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS? There are many other ways to also engage ... For example if we present items to buy and the user buys them, we can the conversion rate
blocks.formula_explanation: This defines CVR as the number of conversions divided by the number of impressions. It measures the fraction of viewed items that lead to a conversion.

blocks.text: Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
But impression does not mean we viewed the item ... We also have
CVS Number of Views or CVS = Number of Conversions Number of Clicks
blocks.nearby_text_before: Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS? But impression does not mean we viewed the item ... We also have
blocks.nearby_text_after: For the above, we can use cookies to track if a user viewed the page, clicked, etc For videos, we might also have average watch time or average completion rate If we present k items per query, all of these metrics might be computed at different k so we can see the effect of results at the top and in the middle of the presented list
blocks.formula_latex: CVS = \frac{\text{Number of Conversions}}{\text{Number of Views}} \quad \text{or} \quad CVS = \frac{\text{Number of Conversions}}{\text{Number of Clicks}}
blocks.formula_explanation: The visible formula gives two possible definitions of CVS: conversions divided by views, or conversions divided by clicks. It is expressing CVS as a ratio-based metric for measuring conversion performance.

blocks.text: = Number of Conversions

blocks.formula_explanation: This is the label for a metric counting how many conversions occurred. The visible content is only the left-hand side of a larger expression, so the full formula is not recoverable from the crop alone.

blocks.text: For the above, we can use cookies to track if a user viewed the page, clicked, etc
For videos, we might also have average watch time or average completion rate
If we present k items per query, all of these metrics might be computed at different k so we can see the effect of results at the top and in the middle of the presented list
The idea is that these metrics are correlated to offline metrics, but the correlation is not always anywhere close to 1.0
Moreover, enough though these are easy to measure, we might be interested in more complicated metrics, such as average watch time not just click though rate
Outline
Recommendation Problems
Metrics
Design Youtube Video Search
Problem
So back to the concrete problem: Given a user and a textual search, we want to present the best videos to this user
Some assumptions
There are 1 M videos
There are 1 M users
We have lots of information about interactions
I.e. When a video is watched we know its name, if it was clicked, finished or what percentage was watched, we know the age of the video, what channel it is displayed in, etc
Problem: Nail down what is going on
Usually, in situations like these you want to ask some questions to clarify ... For example
Question: Is the input text-only, or can users search with an image or video?
Answer: Text queries only
Question: Does the platform serve more than videos?
Answer: Just videos
Question: Do I have information like video title and description?
Answer: Yes; you have this and also the the video content
Question: Do we have training data?
Answer: Yes, let's assume we have about 10 M positive pairs (video id, text query)
Problem: Nail down what is going on
Usually, in situations like this you want to ask some questions to clarify ... For example Video ID 1234
Question: Do we need to personalize results for different users given the same query?
Answer: No, but do the best you can
Summary goal: Design a search system for videos ... The input is a text query, the output is a list of videos that are relevant to the text query
What is our objective?
Our objective is to get the most relevant videos for a user given a textual search

blocks.nearby_text_before: Question: Do we need to personalize results for different users given the same query? Answer: No, but do the best you can Summary goal: Design a search system for videos... The input is a text query, the output is a list of videos that are relevant to the text query
blocks.section_title: Problem: Nail down what is going on
blocks.visual_description: A text query labeled “Wimbledon final 2008” is shown being sent into a video search system, which returns a ranked list of video IDs as results. The content illustrates the basic information-retrieval task of mapping a query to relevant videos.
blocks.figure_focus: Text query to video search retrieval

blocks.text: Strategy and speed considerations
We have a problem ...
For a given search and video (+ features), we need to say which videos would be the top choices (top 10, ranked) and we need to do this fast
'Fast' means we need at a high level a quick way to map a query and some features to a top 10 list of videos
Finally, notice that this is not personalized - we have no user information to worry about
Figuring out how to represent the query and the videos
So given a query, we want to match videos which are similar to this query
How can we represent a query
How can we represent a video?
Representing the query
The query is text, and we know several ways to represent it
We can use Word2Vec - We take the embeddings for each token / word in the query and average over them
We can use SentenceBERT - We take the CLS token and use it to represent the sentence or we average over all the other tokens in the sentence
Which one is better?
Word2Vec - Very quick, but no semantic meaning ... Words like 'bank' will always have the same embeddings and if a sentence is 'a b c d' or 'd c a b' we get the same representation via averaging
BERT - Better, contextual ... But slower ... However, we can use variants of DistilBERT which are small and still give strong representations
Representing the videos
Videos have a text part and a video part
For the text part, we can use any of the methods we used for the query
For the video part, there are a few options, but we want to summarize videos via an embedding
One idea: consume the entire video, and create an embedding to summarize it
This idea uses 3D convolutions and Transformer models
This is expensive, as we need to consume the entire video
Representing the videos
Frame Embedding 1
Another idea to represent the videos:
Another idea: grab frames and use these frames to represent the video Image Model
At a high level, this grabs frames, processes each image with a CNN or some other Transformer, and then averages the embeddings of the frames to get the overall embedding for the video

blocks.nearby_text_before: Another idea to represent the videos: Another idea: grab frames and use these frames to represent the video Image Model At a high level, this grabs frames, processes each image with a CNN or some other Transformer, and then averages the embeddings of the frames to get the overall embedding for the video
blocks.visual_description: A video is split into multiple frames, each frame is passed through an image model to produce frame embeddings, and the embeddings are then aggregated into a single video embedding. The diagram illustrates frame-based video representation using per-frame feature extraction followed by pooling or averaging across frames.
blocks.figure_focus: Frame embeddings aggregated into a video embedding

blocks.text: Making sure the representations are close
For the query and the text representations of the video, what do we know?
Well, we know that generally similar query - text descriptions have high cosine similarity and low ones have a low similarity
This was the idea of SentenceBERT and we saw that shallow models like Word2Vec also are OK to represent sentences
What about the video embeddings vs the query search embedding?
Making sure the representations are close
Given query text, we have a model that produces an embedding
Given some video frames, we have a model that produces embeddings
The above two models are different, and we need to modify these models to yield embedding such that
If the video and the query are the same, the inner product is large
If the video and the query are different, the inner product is low
What do we have and how can we use this?
Making sure the representations are close
We have a set of ordered pairs { ( q i , v i ) } N i =1 where each query is associated with a video (we collect such data or synthesize such data) CNN v1 * q2 VS

blocks.text: How do we use these embeddings
So now we have
Video embeddings which are close to query embeddings when appropriate ... We've placed video and text embeddings in the same space
Text embeddings which are naturally close / far now from similar and dissimilar embeddings
2 SentenceBERT Models?
What else? Well, we need to be careful ...
Notice that we've changed SentenceBERT such that now a query embedding is close/far from a video embedding but
We need to make sure that the 'old' properties of the SentenceBERT model still hold: similar queries have similar embeddings and dissimilar ones have low inner product
To make things even easier, we can have a normalization layer above so that we don't work with inner product but cosine similarity ... I.e. the embeddings models produced are normalized
2 SentenceBERT Models?
For the SentenceBERT issue we can
Have a complex objective which still preserves the textual properties we started with
For example, have the objective or data have query-video pairs and query-query pairs
Make another copy of SentenceBERT and fine tune just this one as above ... This way, in some sense, a query has a 'text' embedding and a 'text-video' embedding
For this last idea, the query travels through both models
How do we use these embeddings
Each query text can get 'two' embeddings ...

blocks.section_title: Each query text can get 'two' embeddings...

The visual contrasts two embedding outputs for the same query, illustrating representation learning for retrieval alignment.

blocks.text: How do we use the embeddings?
Crucially, note that for the videos we can have a service which computes text and video embedding and we can store these vectors them in a database
At run time, we just compute the query's embedding(s)
For a query, we get videos which are associated with the query-video text and and query-video content similarities
For example, we can score videos using a combination of q ⊺ text v text and q ⊺ video v video

blocks.text: Re-Ranking? What else?
In the last picture you see a ranking step
The idea of this type of design is that we have a huge number of videos to select, and we first want to focus on retrieval and high Recall so that we get a set of relevant videos for our query
Now, once we have a manageable set of size 100 (say), we can use a heavier model to refine our results with ranking ...
At the ranking stage, we focus on Precision as the key metric
Anything before we rank? What else?
The architecture we used was nice for a few reasons
Essentially, we used a BiEncoder for relevance - this is a Two-Tower architecture which produces embeddings such that a search embedding is close to the video embedding
blocks.nearby_text_before: Anything before we rank? What else? The architecture we used was nice for a few reasons
blocks.nearby_text_after: This type of architecture is good because we can cache / save embedding and at runtime we just need the query embedding A set of videos might be huge and a standard search O ( N ) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in O (log N ) time

A set of videos might be huge and a standard search O ( N ) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in O (log N ) time

blocks.text: Candidate Generator and kNN
At this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos
Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding
blocks.nearby_text_before: Candidate Generator and kNN At this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos
blocks.nearby_text_after: Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user
blocks.text_raw: Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding
blocks.text_cleaned: Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding

blocks.text: Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v
Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product

The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user
Specifically, if there are | V | videos and each vector has dimensions d , finding the top k will be O ( | V | log kd ) (use a max heap)

blocks.nearby_text_after: So, we'll need a way to trade off the accuracy of the nearest neighbors search in favor of a reduced latency... We need to get rid of the | V |

blocks.text_cleaned: So, we'll need a way to trade off the accuracy of the nearest neighbors search in favor of a reduced latency ... We need to get rid of the |V|

blocks.text: LSH kNN idea
This type of issue falls under the umbrella of approximate nearest neighbor search
The main idea of most approximate kNN algorithms revolves around breaking up the embedding space into regions, for example, locality sensitive hashing (LSH)
The main idea of LSH is to partition the embeddings by their similarities and then when searching for nearest neighbors, look only at the partition that the input example falls into
At runtime, this prevents the need to search through all of the video embeddings and instead isolates the search to a partition which will contain similar embeddings / videos

blocks.nearby_text_before: The main idea of most approximate kNN algorithms revolves around breaking up the embedding space into regions, for example, locality sensitive hashing (LSH) The main idea of LSH is to partition the embeddings by their similarities and then when searching for nearest neighbors, look only at the partition that the input example falls into At runtime, this prevents the need to search through all of the video embeddings and instead isolates the search to a partition which will contain similar embeddings / videos
blocks.visual_description: A query point is projected into a partitioned embedding space divided by several slanted boundary lines, with nearby vectors labeled v1, v4, and v5 inside one region. The illustration shows the nearest-neighbor search idea of checking only the partition containing the query rather than scanning all embeddings.
blocks.figure_focus: LSH partition-based approximate kNN search

blocks.text: How does kNN work?
We have | V | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O ( | V | ) which is too expensive
blocks.nearby_text_before: How does kNN work?
blocks.nearby_text_after: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval High level ideas? Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket
blocks.text_raw: We have | V | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O ( | V | ) which is too expensive
blocks.text_cleaned: We have |V| videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O(|V|) which is too expensive

blocks.text: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval

blocks.text_raw: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval
blocks.text_cleaned: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O(log |V|) thereby allowing much faster retrieval

blocks.text: High level ideas?
Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash ... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket
Tree based methods also work, and they partition the space of videos into regions ... At runtime, the query is projected to a region and similarly we just consider videos in that region
Finally, we can also group videos by similarity via clustering ... At runtime, we look at the cluster the query belongs to and then just search that cluster
For all the ideas above, the methods are tested and available to you, so you can use things like FAISS or SCANN quite easily ... The error rates on these algorithms is quite low
Re-Ranking? What else?
Some ideas on the final ranker

At this step, we can also use a CrossEncoder so that for each candidate video we get a score when we feed in the pair ( q , v ) where q is the query v is the video ... We do this for all videos we need to consider

For example, if we have 100 videos we might break up the 100 into batches of size 10 feed these batches to servers which host the CrossEncoders and then get 100 scores and rank the videos based on these scores
Here, we batch since we want to distribute the load but this might not be necessary with the right model + GPU
I.e. if the model large, we might need this ... If it is small, we might not
Re-Ranking with a CrossEncoder and more

blocks.section_title: Re-Ranking with a CrossEncoder and more
blocks.visual_description: A query and a video are both fed into a Cross Encoder to produce a query-video CrossEncoder score. That score is then combined with outputs from other feature models to produce a final score used for ranking.
blocks.figure_focus: CrossEncoder-based re-ranking with additional feature models

blocks.text: Re-Ranking? What else?
Some ideas on the final ranker
For each video, we also know features such as: historical CTR EMA for 3, 5, 7 days, EMA of likes for 3, 5, 7 days, differences between EMAs, age, topic, etc
We could also have a MLP which takes in the query embedding, the video embedding, a set of normalized video features, and finally produces a score (maybe a probability of click)?
This is another idea but notice that we have a lot of features here but many fewer videos

There are other cases: we can have multiple rakers where the rankers get more and more 'heavy' (i.e. first we have a Logistic Regression, then an MLP / CrossEncoder hybrid but this last ranker runs on very few videos)
After ranking, we can even have another phase that boosts new videos up, ensures diversity, etc - these could be heuristic rules
This last step is crucial also!
What if we wanted personalization?
In this case, for two users who search the same query, we expect that the answers might not be the same ... I.e. We want to return answers that depend on the user ID
In this case, then, we need to build for each user a feature vector so that we can incorporate information into the model's ranking
One idea: We have a system such that for a search query we return a set of relevant videos ...
We could ...
Use our previous system to retrieve a set of relevant videos regardless of the user
At the ranking step, we can impute user features such as age, location, sex, historical CTR, historical logins etc and use these features to rank videos
But, ideally, we don't just do this at ranking time ... Ideally we have a set of user embeddings per user and we use this at retrieval time
What if we wanted personalization? More topics for next time ...
We'll need a way to figure out user embeddings
What about new users? This is the cold-start problem
For our previous analysis, we could have new videos but we just need to push these through the CNN and save the embeddings
Moreover, if we are not sure, we can use some sort of heuristic .. For example:
Find the topic of the video
Use the average video embedding across videos in this topic
What about bias?
What if the model is large?
What about click-bait videos?
What is Sparse search? I.e. How else can we search beyond just using embeddings and why should we even bother?

blocks.text: What happens if you ask an aligned LLM a question?
dent of the united states in 2006 was," is says concisely:
George W. Bush was the president of the United States in 2006. He served two terms in office, from January 20, 2001, to January 20, 2009.
How would you align an LLM?
Supervised Fine Tuning
So, like in HW but at a much bigger scale, we can build a Transformer based (Decoder) Language Model
Given another question question like 'Tell me who the president is?', how might a LLM like GPT3 answer? It would babble on
We can leave GPT3 alone and see what it generates, or, we can give it examples like the above and have it 'understand' what to do given such a prompt
Specifically, we aim to give the model a prompt like 'You are a helpful chatbot. Question: Tell me who the president is. Answer: ' and have it generate ' Donald Trump'.
Specifically, we can have a loss on 'Donald Trump STOP' and feed it 'START Donald Trump' ... Note the start token and the stop token STOP
Note, if we do not do this, and just have the model generate text, it might just generate the questions again. Or the answer, or whatever (we saw this with another example)
Supervised Fine Tuning Schematic
Generally, the task here is very similar to what we saw for classification before STAGE 1 STAGE 3
With classification, you want to get a score from an input sentence and sampling Classifier
Here, you input text and you want the model to generate text that answers your question
pretrained an LLM.

Arrows and numbered steps connect the stages to show how pretraining leads into fine-tuning for task-specific behavior.

blocks.text: Supervised Fine Tuning Examples
inputs for the LLI

< a desired response.
Edit the following sentence to song was composed by the artist."

Supervised Fine Tuning Output
### Instruction:
Once we train the model, we need to evaluate the quality of the generation vs a known response
Your model might be small and specific (spell corrector), and can user a large LLM for this
Model response:
>> The car is as fast as a bullet.
Getting a Personal Assistant
Is SFT enough?
Using SFT there are a few key limitations:
Data reflects human outputs, not preferences
You're training the model to mimic what a human might say, but not to choose among many plausible options based on what humans prefer
What about ambiguity and multiple good answers
Often, there's more than one good answer to a prompt
SFT only shows one per example, which may bias the model away from equally good or better answers
SFT doesn't capture what people like vs what's merely correct
Just because something is factually right doesn't mean it's helpful, kind, clear, or engaging
SFT doesn't optimize for human satisfaction or alignment with human values
Getting a Personal Assistant
For example, suppose we have an LLM and we've fine tuned it to generally add two numbers
... Suppose we want to build an assistant to help us do calculations and understand how to do them ... We have a question: 'What is 2 + 2*3?'
blocks.nearby_text_before: Getting a Personal Assistant For example, suppose we have an LLM and we've fine tuned it to generally add two numbers
blocks.nearby_text_after: We might get two generations like below: Generation 1: '8' Generation 2: 'First, I want to use the laws of operations to calculate 2*3 and get 6. Then, I add 2 and get 8.'
blocks.text_raw: ... Suppose we want to build an assistant to help us do calculations and understand how to do them ... We have a question: 'What is 2 + 2*3?'
blocks.text_cleaned: ... Suppose we want to build an assistant to help us do calculations and understand how to do them ... We have a question: 'What is $2 + 2*3?%'

blocks.text: We might get two generations like below:
Generation 1: '8'
Generation 2: 'First, I want to use the laws of operations to calculate 2*3 and get 6. Then, I add 2 and get 8.'
Neither of the above is wrong, but we want to entice the model to generate like the second answer ... How might we do that?
So, to review, to get a personal assistant we need a few things
Step 1: Build a general LLM
For this part, we will just train a Transformer for next token prediction
To get this working, you process data so that generally ( y 0 , . . . , y t -1 ) is used as the input and ( y 1 , . . . , y t ) is used as the output
blocks.nearby_text_before: Step 1: Build a general LLM For this part, we will just train a Transformer for next token prediction
blocks.nearby_text_after: The above is generally now a Transformer decoder, but it can be any model; Transformers are used because you can scale and they do very well For the above you still need to determine the right data to use You need quality data...
blocks.text_raw: To get this working, you process data so that generally ( y 0 , . . . , y t -1 ) is used as the input and ( y 1 , . . . , y t ) is used as the output
blocks.text_cleaned: To get this working, you process data so that generally (y_0, ..., y_{t-1}) is used as the input and (y_1, ..., y_t) is used as the output

blocks.text: The above is generally now a Transformer decoder, but it can be any model; Transformers are used because you can scale and they do very well
For the above you still need to determine the right data to use
You need quality data ...
You still need to know the number of parameters vs your compute budget
You need to carefully do the optimization ...
Etc.
The idea of this step is we need a general model that understand language well
Step 2: Fine tune to your use case
Here, the idea is we want a model that understands that given a format of examples it should turn around and answer specifically the questions we address it
At this step, we take examples ( x , y 0 , . . . , y t -1 ) as input and have targets ( y 1 , . . . , y t )
Examples at this step are like the ones we saw before in the previous slides
Step 3: Refine the model - Reinforcement Learning!
Here, we want to have the model generally output y w over y l given x
In this case, y w and y l might be somewhat correct but given x as input y w is deemed better and we want to entice the model to generate y w
Many applications for this ...
Addition: tell is a step-by-step answer
Block the model: x ='How do I build a bomb?' ... Don't answer!
Step 3: Refine the model
How might we do this step?
Suppose we have a reward r ∗ ( x , y ) which we give to the model for generating y given x - this is the Human reward which we do not know

blocks.text: Bradley-Terry Model for rewards
We can assume that human preference is written as
P ∗ (y w > y l | x) = e r ∗ (x, yw) e r ∗ (x, yw) + e r ∗ (x, yl) = σ (r ∗ (x, y w) -r ∗ (x, y l))
blocks.nearby_text_before: Bradley-Terry Model for rewards We can assume that human preference is written as
blocks.nearby_text_after: The above is the Bradley-Terry model which connects the probability of y w > y l with the optimal reward r ∗ We do not know r ∗ , we can estimate it as r ϕ or just see what r ∗ yields if we do some math (more later) Goal: I want to get π θ ( y | x ) which puts more probability on y w than y l ... This is the same model as π ref ( y | x ) but modified / aligned with human feedback for this task
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the probability that winner response $y_w$ is preferred over loser response $y_l$ given $x$. It expresses that probability both as a softmax over the two rewards and as a sigmoid of their reward difference.

blocks.text: KL = -E p [log q (y) p (y)] ≥ -log E p [q (y) p (y)] = -log ∑ y q (y) = -log 1 = 0

blocks.nearby_text_after: So, KL Divergence is a positive metric It can be shown that KL is zero if an only if p and q are the same Generally, we can use the KL Divergence as a type of 'regularizer' to make sure one distribution is 'close' to some base distribution
blocks.section_title: KL Divergence for any two distributions p and q
blocks.formula_latex: KL = -E p [log q(y) p(y)] ≥ -log E p [q(y) p(y)] = -log \sum y q(y) = -log 1 = 0
blocks.formula_explanation: This inequality shows that the KL divergence is always at least zero. It uses Jensen’s inequality on the convex function \(-\log x\), and the final step gives \(-\log \sum_y q(y) = -\log 1 = 0\).

blocks.text: So, KL Divergence is a positive metric
It can be shown that KL is zero if an only if p and q are the same
Generally, we can use the KL Divergence as a type of 'regularizer' to make sure one distribution is 'close' to some base distribution
Back to Step 3; a Reinforcement Learning objective
Consider the objective
J (θ) = max θ, x ∼ D, y ∼ π θ (y | x) E [r ∗ (x, y)] -β KL [π θ (y | x) || π ref (y | x)]
blocks.nearby_text_before: Back to Step 3; a Reinforcement Learning objective Consider the objective
blocks.nearby_text_after: Typically, problems like this are done with Reinforcement Learning We have a reward We have a policy π
blocks.section_title: Back to Step 3; a Reinforcement Learning objective
blocks.formula_latex: J(\theta)=\max_{\theta,\,x\sim D,\,y\sim\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}\,E[r^*(x,y)]-\beta KL[\pi_\theta(y\mid x)\|\pi_{ref}(y\mid x)]
blocks.formula_explanation: This objective maximizes the expected reward term while subtracting a KL-divergence penalty to keep the learned policy close to a reference policy. The maximization is taken over samples of \(x\sim D\) and \(y\sim\pi_\theta(y\mid x)\).

blocks.text: Typically, problems like this are done with Reinforcement Learning
We have a reward
We have a policy π
We want to get the optimal policy which gives us the best reward
What is the above trying to do?
β > 0, with larger values making it harder and harder to move away from π ref ( y | x )

blocks.visual_description: The figure explains the standard RLHF workflow in three steps: first collect demonstration data to train a supervised policy, then collect comparison data to train a reward model, and finally optimize the policy with reinforcement learning using the reward model. It shows prompts, human-labeled preferred outputs, a reward model, and PPO-based policy updates.
blocks.figure_focus: Standard RLHF training loop

Left part: RLHF
Right part: alignment without Reinforcement Learning (DPO)

In contrast, DPO directly optimizes for the policy best satisfying the preferences with a simple classification objective, fitting an implicit reward model whose corresponding optimal policy can be extracted in closed form.

blocks.nearby_text_before: x: "write me a poem about the history of jazz" preference data label rewards In contrast, DPO directly optimizes for the policy best satisfying the preferences with a simple classification objective, fitting an implicit reward model whose corresponding optimal policy can be extracted in closed form.

blocks.visual_description: A comparison of reinforcement learning from human feedback shows preference data for a prompt, a reward model trained by maximum likelihood, and an LM policy updated by labeled rewards in a reinforcement learning loop. The prompt example is "write me a poem about the history of jazz," illustrating how feedback is used to train the policy through the reward model.
blocks.figure_focus: RLHF reward model and policy optimization loop

blocks.nearby_text_before: x: "write me a poem about the history of jazz" preference data label rewards In contrast, DPO directly optimizes for the policy best satisfying the preferences with a simple classification objective, fitting an implicit reward model whose corresponding optimal policy can be extracted in closed form.
blocks.nearby_text_after: x: "write me a poem about the history of jazz" preference data final LM
blocks.visual_description: Direct Preference Optimization is illustrated as taking preference data consisting of a preferred and a rejected response for the prompt "write me a poem about the history of jazz" and optimizing the model with a maximum-likelihood objective. The process maps the preference pairs to the final language model, emphasizing that DPO learns the policy directly rather than separately fitting an explicit reward model.

It encourages the model to rank the preferred output above the less preferred one.

blocks.text: At optimality, we hope to converge to π ∗ having started at π θ
The above model is called Direct Preference Optimization
At a high level, this is a Reinforcement Learning alternative which has no modeled reward function
This encourages the model to assign higher probability to preferred outputs - no explicit reward function is needed, but the loss function implicitly reflects preference
DPO uses human preference data directly to optimize a contrastive loss between a preferred output and a less preferred one
... You could say the log-probabilities of preferred vs dispreferred outputs act as a proxy reward
DPO is effectively matching the behavior of an implicit reward function that ranks outputs based on preference
DPO Caveats with Implementation

DPO Issues?
Issues with DPO?

Assumption Mismatch .. DPO is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values. But real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent. If the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data

Implicit Reward Model ... DPO skips the explicit reward model used in RLHF and optimizes policy directly. This is great for simplicity but means you can't inspect, analyze, or reuse a separate reward model, which can be valuable for understanding model behavior or for multi-stage training setups ... I can't really see the reward even thought I know it is connected to r ∗
blocks.nearby_text_before: Issues with DPO? Assumption Mismatch.. DPO is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values. But real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent. If the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data
blocks.nearby_text_after: Sensitivity to Preference Quality... DPO uses pairwise comparisons (e.g., A > B) to guide training. If those preferences are sparse, inconsistent, or biased, the optimization can go astray. There's no reinforcement loop to 'pull back' the model like in PPO (used in RLHF) Less Control Over Output Distribution... Because DPO skips reinforcement-style feedback loops, it may offer less control over certain safety and behavioral constraints that can be more finely tuned with RLHF (e.g., toxicity reduction via explicit reward shaping) Lack of Exploration... In RLHF (especially with PPO), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback. DPO, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it

Lack of Exploration ... In RLHF (especially with PPO), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback. DPO, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it
Why use RLHF?
Benefits of RLHF?
Explicit Reward Modeling ... RLHF gives you a separate reward model, which can be valuable for:
Auditing what the model 'likes'
Reusing it across models
Improving interpretability

Fine-Grained Control via Reward Tuning ... Want your model to be funny and safe and informative? RLHF lets you weight these reward components explicitly and dynamically adjust the mix ... There is no 'optimal' implicit reward

Better Performance in Complex or High-Stakes Domains ... In cases where getting nuanced behavior is critical (like legal or medical advice), RLHF might better capture and enforce complex preference structures (+ rewards)

Proven Track Record ... Many high-profile models (like ChatGPT and Claude) use RLHF because it's a mature and battle-tested method. If you're aiming for production-level robustness, RLHF is still the gold standard
Is there anything other than DPO?
Alternatives to DPO
There are a few DPO alternatives ... Ask Chat GPT for some papers!
Big idea is to change some of the final optimization but sometimes there is no nice analytic derivation ...

Retrieval Augmented Generation
Given a model like GPT3 trained up to 2023, how do we answer the question 'Who is the current president?'
Given this question, the LLM will give an answer but the answer (because of data cutoff) would be 'Joe Biden'
This is an example of Hallucination; in this case the model gives back a confident answer but it is wrong because its data is not fresh enough
Retraining the model is very expensive and fine tuning for language modeling might break some old information
Moreover, the model weights might not even be available (GPT3, GPT4 etc)

blocks.text: To retrieve chunks, you can use a BiEncoder
Offline, documents are chunked and the chunks are embedded by the BiEncoder and stored in a database
At runtime the query is encoded and the chunks with the highest inner product against the query are retrieved
For a query, you get the documents which have the highest cosine similarity in the embedding space
Using brute force, this is O ( N )

blocks.formula_explanation: This identity shows the mean squared error of \(\hat{\mu}\) decomposed into squared bias plus variance. In the visible case, the estimator is unbiased, so the bias term is zero and the MSE equals \(\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\mu})\).

MSE = E ((ˆ µ -µ) 2) = σ 2 N

blocks.formula_explanation: The expression gives the mean squared error of the estimator \(\hat{\mu}\) around the true mean \(\mu\). It states that, for this unbiased estimator, the MSE equals the variance term \(\sigma^2/N\).

log(MSE) = 2 log σ -log N

blocks.formula_explanation: The visible formula states that the logarithm of the mean squared error equals twice the logarithm of \sigma minus the logarithm of N. It expresses how the error scales with the noise level \sigma and sample size N.

Chinchilla Vs Other Models
What about for LLMs?

At a high level, the authors establish empirical rules which connect the Error or Loss with the model parameter count and the training dataset size Jurassic (Lieber et al., 2021) Chinchilla 178 Billion 300 Billion
Why does this matter?
Smaller models have less inference latency
Smaller models are easier to load and use
Smaller models are 'easier to train'; we just need lots of data but less GPUs
Also, it is nice to understand when a model is 'baked' ... When should we stop training a model?

blocks.nearby_text_before: Why does this matter? Smaller models have less inference latency Smaller models are easier to load and use

It highlights that Chinchilla has 70 billion parameters but was trained on 1.4 trillion tokens, contrasting with larger models such as GPT-3, Jurassic, Gopher, and MT-NLG that were trained on roughly 300 billion tokens.

blocks.text: Scaling Laws
Key question: What is compute cost?
Compute Cost = FLOPs
FLOPs: Floating Point Operations per second
Sort of like big O notation, used to measure work
Adding two floats costs 1
Multiplying two floats costs 1
What else?
FLOPs Calculations for Matrices
Suppose we have two vectors a and b of dimension d
How many FLOPs do we need to compute a ⊺ b ?
a ⊺ b = ∑ d i =1 a i b i

blocks.formula_focus: Optimal N and D under compute budget
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines the pair of model size and data size that minimize loss subject to a fixed FLOPs budget C. It selects the best N,D combination among those with total compute equal to C.

blocks.text: In the above, L is the (Test) loss from the model and C is the compute budget, which is the total number of FLOPs
Optimization Problem and Implications
We want to fix C and solve
N ∗ (C), D ∗ (C) = argmin {FLOPs (N, D)= C} L (N, D)

blocks.text: Experiment 1: Fix model size N , train each for various number of tokens D - 108 101)
For the first experiment the authors do the following
They fix models of different sizes, considering a range of model from 70 M to 10 B total parameters N
For each model, train using 4 different setting of tokens D
As we train longer, we get different compute budgets
Question: Given a compute budget, which model gets the lowest loss?

blocks.section_title: Experiment 3: Analytic formula for the Loss
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives a fitted estimate of the loss \(\hat{L}(N,D)\) as a baseline term plus two inverse power-law terms in \(N\) and \(D\). It models how the loss changes with the two variables using parameters \(E, A, B, \alpha,\beta\).

blocks.visual_description: The figure compares language-model loss as a function of training compute and model size. The left plot shows iso-loss contours over training FLOPs versus model size, with an efficient frontier and empirical data points indicating how loss changes across compute budgets; the right plot slices loss by model size for fixed FLOP budgets, showing U-shaped curves and the best-performing region shifting with compute. The overall message is that optimal model size depends on available training FLOPs, and loss can be approximated analytically from this scaling relationship.
blocks.figure_focus: Iso-loss and compute-optimal model scaling

blocks.visual_description: A log-log scaling plot relates training tokens to model parameters under three predicted approaches, with lines labeled Approach 1, Approach 2, and Approach 3. Several large language models are marked as star points above the scaling curves, including Chinchilla, Gopher, GPT-3, and Megatron-Turing NLG, showing that these models sit at different positions relative to the predicted optimal scaling relationship.
blocks.figure_focus: Model parameter-token scaling law comparison

blocks.text: What about BERT?
Recall BERT Large was 340 M parameters (v1.1/2.0)
It was trained on BooksCorpus (800 M words) and English Wikipedia (2500 M words) BERTLAROR
Each word has about 1.25 tokens after tokenization, so we have about 4.125 B unique tokens
BERT trained for 1 M steps with batch size 256 and each row had 512 tokens
So BERT Large was trained on 131 B tokens but only 4.125 B of them were unique
BERT went over the same tokens roughly 30 times

blocks.nearby_text_before: It was trained on BooksCorpus (800 M words) and English Wikipedia (2500 M words) BERTLAROR Each word has about 1.25 tokens after tokenization, so we have about 4.125 B unique tokens BERT trained for 1 M steps with batch size 256 and each row had 512 tokens
blocks.visual_description: A comparison table reports benchmark results for RoBERTa, BERT-large, and XLNet-large under different training data and training-step settings. Columns include data size, batch size, steps, and performance on SQuAD v1.1/2.0, MNLI-m, and SST-2, showing that more data and longer pretraining generally improve scores.
blocks.figure_focus: Pretraining scale comparison across BERT-family models

blocks.text: What about ROBERTA?
One of the nuances of the Chinchilla paper is that the unique token sequences are the ones pushing your model up the most ... If you keep going too much over the same tokens you don't get much
ROBERTA trained on more data (160 GB vs 16 GB so about 30 B tokens) and introduced dynamic masking and other optimization tricks so that repeated data was used better
Also, it trained for far longer, thereby getting better performance

blocks.nearby_text_before: One of the nuances of the Chinchilla paper is that the unique token sequences are the ones pushing your model up the most... If you keep going too much over the same tokens you don't get much ROBERTA trained on more data (160 GB vs 16 GB so about 30 B tokens) and introduced dynamic masking and other optimization tricks so that repeated data was used better

The table shows that adding more data and training longer improve SQuAD, MNLI-m, and SST-2 scores, with RoBERTa outperforming BERT large and XLNet large in the listed settings.
blocks.figure_focus: RoBERTa gains from more data and longer pretraining

Vs Gopher, Chinchilla does much better on all Language Modeling tasks
This is not always the case since lowest Loss does not mean best downstream, but generally there is a positive correlation here
Chinchilla outperforms Gopher.

blocks.section_title: Chinchilla vs Gopher on Language Modeling
blocks.visual_description: A bar chart compares the decrease in bpb relative to Gopher across several language modeling benchmarks. Most datasets show modest improvements, while the largest gains appear on EuroParl and Gutenberg/Pg_19, indicating Chinchilla performs better than Gopher on these tasks.
blocks.figure_focus: Chinchilla vs Gopher benchmark improvements

blocks.text: Chinchilla vs Gopher on MMLU tasks
Vs Gopher, a much larger model the compute-optimal Chinchilla does a lot better even on indirect tasks
This type of behavior was present across many tasks
So - you don't need just a larger model, you need a well trained one!

Specifically, if I have a model w N parameters, do I just keep going over and over the same data and making gains? No, I need new data. And No, at some point even new data will not be optimal, for that FLOPs budget I should train a larger model on an appropriate amount of data 2/57, and worse on only 4/57 tasks.

blocks.text: A Longer Computational Graph, but still Logistic Regression

blocks.section_title: A Longer Computational Graph, but still Logistic Regression
blocks.visual_description: The graph shows a two-layer logistic regression computation: the input x produces a hidden activation a[1] through z[1] = β[1]a[0] + α[1] and a[1] = Θ z[1], then a second affine transform z[2] = β[2]a[1] + α[2] produces a[2] = Θ z[2]. The final node computes the logistic loss ℓ = y log a[2] + (1 - y) log(1 - a[2]) using the prediction a[2] and target y.
blocks.figure_focus: Longer logistic-regression computation graph

blocks.text: Back To Logistic Regression
Let a [ l ] be the nonlinear transformation of a layer l , so that a [ l ] = f [ l ] ( z [ l ] ) where f [ l ] is a nonlinearity at layer l

blocks.text: Logistic Regression and NN
Note that Logistic Regression can be views as a '1-layer' Neural Network
The big step between the Logistic model and the Neural Network is the additional nonlinearity introduced by the ReLU application
This application, by the way, makes the model nonconvex: it is a much harder to find
max β,α ℓ (β, α)

blocks.formula_explanation: The expression is the maximization of a function \(\ell(\beta,\alpha)\) over the parameters \(\beta\) and \(\alpha\). It indicates an optimization objective, likely selecting parameter values that make \(\ell\) as large as possible.

blocks.text: What is the computational graph?
MLP Computational Graph

blocks.visual_description: A feedforward multilayer perceptron computation is shown with input x propagating through two affine layers and sigmoid activations to produce a final activation a[2]. The loss is a binary cross-entropy expression, ℓ = y log a[2] + (1 - y) log(1 - a[2]), with the label y feeding into the loss node. Bias terms β[1], α[1], β[2], and α[2] are included in the layer computations.
blocks.figure_focus: MLP forward computation and binary cross-entropy loss

blocks.text: Visually looking at Activations

blocks.visual_description: Several common neural-network activation functions are shown with their formulas and characteristic output curves: sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, Maxout, and ELU. The curves illustrate how each function transforms input values, including saturation for sigmoid and tanh, piecewise linear behavior for ReLU variants, and a smooth negative-region correction for ELU.
blocks.figure_focus: Common neural network activation functions

Word Embeddings Motivation
Given : A corpus C with sentences
Want : A vector representation for each word a w such that the vector a w captures some of the 'meaning' of w for each w
Ideally : These vectors have nice mathematical properties
If two words w 1 , w 2 are related, their vectors are related in the sense that a ⊺ w 1 a w 2 is high

blocks.text: Why Word Embeddings: Motivation
But Why? ... There are a few textbook answers:

Traditional approaches treated words as independent entities, ignoring their meanings and relationships. Word embeddings, however, represent words as dense vectors in a continuous space, capturing semantic similarities (e.g., 'king' and 'queen' are close in the embedding space)
One-hot encoding creates sparse, high-dimensional vectors (with a dimension for every unique word), which leads to inefficiencies in computation and storage
Pretrained embeddings can be used across various NLP applications, reducing the need for large labeled datasets
Tasks like sentiment analysis, machine translation, and named entity recognition benefit from embeddings, as they provide richer word representations than simple frequency-based methods
Models like Word2Vec utilize context to learn word meanings. This helps in better distinguishing between usage (e.g.,'river bank' vs. 'money bank')
For Word2Vec, we actually always have 1 embedding for a word, so averaging the two embeddings above is crucial
Word Embeddings: From Str to Int
Tokenize C in some way
Possible recipe: Take the tokens as the words
Take C and split it on spaces
Eliminate rare words?
Collapse some words together (running, run, runs)?
We now have a vocabulary set V with a set of words { a, and, the, cat, mat, ... }
Each word can be assigned a unique integer, and we have a hash map stoi = { w → i }

blocks.text: Word Embeddings Motivation: One-Hot Problems
If V is a large set, the vectors we get for each word are very large
The notion of 'similarity' is lost, each vector is independent
Specifically, for any two different words i and j we have
a ⊺ wi a wj = 0

SVD Has Problems
Some problems:
X is very sparse
Expensive cost to train: O ( |V| 3 )
blocks.nearby_text_before: SVD Has Problems X is very sparse
blocks.nearby_text_after: New words imply that the dimension of the matrix changes very often Very large matrix! X usually is not just co-occurrence counts, we usually need more than this and some hacks to make this work best (so the rows of A capture as much semantic and syntactic information as possible)
blocks.text_raw: Expensive cost to train: O ( |V| 3 )
blocks.text_cleaned: Expensive cost to train: O(|V|^3)

blocks.text: New words imply that the dimension of the matrix changes very often
Very large matrix!
X usually is not just co-occurrence counts, we usually need more than this and some hacks to make this work best (so the rows of A capture as much semantic and syntactic information as possible)
Can use Pearson correlation instead of counts
Remove function words like 'he', 'has', 'the', 'a', etc.
Use a ramp window: weigh co-occurence matrix by distance instead of just counts
Instead of this, let's try something else!
Word Embeddings Motivation: Towards Word2Vec
Idea: Instead of computing global statistics, we create a model that learns at each iteration and refines what it learns as it progresses
The main paper (from 2013) below has the core ideas we look at next
Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
After this paper, there were a few generalization and followups but we will probably skip them (ask me if interested!)
These Methods: static word embeddings, where each word has 1 representation
For example, with s 1 ='On the river bank' and s 2 ='I robbed the bank' there is only 1 vector for 'bank'
BERT and Modern Embeddings: words don't have 1 embedding (in the above, 'bank' gets a different representation depending on its context)

Outline
Basic Word Embeddings
CBOW Model
Skip-Gram Model
Word2Vec At A High Level
A high level summary:
2 models: CBOW and Skip-Gram
CBOW: predict a center word from the words around it
Skip-Gram: predict words around a center word
2 tricks: Negative Sampling and Hierarchical Softmax
Both these methods try to solve for the issue that |V| is a very large and leads to slow optimization as we'll see
Both models are built on the idea: 'You shall know a word by the company it keeps' (Distributional Hypothesis - Harris 1954)
I.e. Words that are close together or used in the same way tend to mean the same thing
Word Embeddings: CBOW
The goal of CBOW is to predict the missing center word from the neighboring context words

blocks.visual_description: Four surrounding context words labeled w_{t-2}, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, and w_{t+2} feed into a central averaged representation w_avg, which then points to the target word w_t. The arrows indicate the CBOW idea of predicting the center word from nearby context words by combining them into one context vector.
