blocks.text: Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
Given a set of documents C , break up C into sentences and tokenize C to get the vocabulary V
Fix a window size m so for each word we look m words to the right and left
Example: if s = 'This is a short sentence.' and m = 1 we get
[' this ' , ' is ' , ' a ']
[' is ' , ' a ' , ' short ']
[' a ' , ' short ' , ' sentence ']
[' short ' , ' sentence ' , ' . ']

Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
The loss of a general output word w o and fixed window around it is:
L (A, B) = -log p (w o | w o -m,..., w o -1, w o +1,..., w o + m) = -log p (w o | a avg) = -log exp b ⊺ wo a avg ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a avg = -b ⊺ wo a avg +log ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a avg
blocks.nearby_text_before: Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm The loss of a general output word w o and fixed window around it is
blocks.nearby_text_after: Generally, the total loss of this model is the loss across the entire training data by windowing in this way and averaging over the number of windows The parameters of this model are A and B We can use SGD to update the parameters of this model
blocks.formula_latex: L(A,B)=-\log p(w_o\mid w_{o-m},\ldots,w_{o-1},w_{o+1},\ldots,w_{o+m})=-\log p(w_o\mid a_{avg})=-\log \frac{\exp(b_{w_o}^\top a_{avg})}{\sum_{w\in V}\exp(b_w^\top a_{avg})}=-b_{w_o}^\top a_{avg}+\log\sum_{w\in V}\exp(b_w^\top a_{avg})
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the CBOW loss for predicting the center word from its surrounding context words. It rewrites the conditional probability as a softmax and then expands the negative log-likelihood into a score term plus a log-sum-exp normalization term.

blocks.text: Generally, the total loss of this model is the loss across the entire training data by windowing in this way and averaging over the number of windows
The parameters of this model are A and B
We can use SGD to update the parameters of this model
A new ← A old -η ∇ A L ( A old , B old )

Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram
Another approach takes the reverse of the CBOW model: given a center word, predict the words around this word
As with CBOW, you have parameter embedding matrix A , B as before

blocks.visual_description: A central token w_t points to several surrounding tokens labeled w_{t+1}, w_{t+2}, w_{t-1}, and w_{t-2}, illustrating the prediction of nearby context words from a target word. The arrows indicate the skip-gram training objective of using the center word to learn representations that capture surrounding context.
blocks.figure_focus: Skip-gram context prediction from center word

blocks.formula_latex: L(A, B) = -m \sum i = -m, i =0 e wc + i log p wc + i
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the loss for predicting each context word around the center word. It sums over offsets from \(-m\) to \(m\), excluding \(0\), using the one-hot target \(e_{w_{c+i}}\) and the predicted probability \(p_{w_{c+i}}\).

blocks.text: Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model
We can spell-out the above more by noticing that in our model we want to minimize:

L (A, B) = -log p (w c -m, w c -m +1,..., w c + m -1, w c + m | w c) = -log m ∏ i = -m, i =0 p (w c + i | w c) = -log m ∏ i = -m, i =0 exp b ⊺ wc + i a wc ∑ |V| j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc = -m ∑ i = -m, i =0 b ⊺ wc + i a wc +2 m log |V| ∑ j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc
blocks.nearby_text_before: Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model We can spell-out the above more by noticing that in our model we want to minimize
blocks.formula_latex: L(A, B) = -log p(w c -m, w c -m +1, \ldots, w c + m -1, w c + m | w c) = -log m \prod i = -m, i =0 p(w c + i | w c) = -log m \prod i = -m, i =0 exp b^\top wc + i a wc \sum | V | j =1 exp b^\top wj a wc = -m \sum i = -m, i =0 b^\top wc + i a wc +2 m log | V | \sum_{j=1} exp b^\top wj a wc
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the skip-gram objective as the negative log probability of the context words around a center word. It is expanded into a product of conditional probabilities and then into a sum involving dot products and a softmax normalizer over the vocabulary.

blocks.formula_explanation: This expression defines a negative log-likelihood summed over the context window around the center word. It adds the log probabilities of each surrounding word given the center word, excluding the center position itself.

blocks.text: is the sum of 2 m terms
m ∑ i = -m, i =0 Cross -Entropy (e c + i, softmax (Ba wc))
blocks.nearby_text_before: Note that in the above, we've used the Naive Bayes' assumption p ( A , B | C ) = p ( A | C ) p ( B | C ) repeatedly Note that in actuality we have that is the sum of 2 m terms
blocks.formula_latex: m \sum i = -m, i =0 Cross -Entropy(e c + i, softmax(Ba wc))
blocks.formula_explanation: The expression sums the cross-entropy loss over context offsets from \(-m\) to \(m\), excluding \(i=0\). It compares the target context vector \(e_{c+i}\) with the softmax output \(\mathrm{softmax}(Ba\,w_c)\).

blocks.text: Skip-Gram Gradients
So, a general term of the Skip-Gram objective looks like
L (A, B) = -log p (b wo | a wc)) = -b ⊺ wo a wc +log ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a wc
blocks.nearby_text_before: Skip-Gram Gradients So, a general term of the Skip-Gram objective looks like
blocks.nearby_text_after: We have (HW!) Notice that this is E [ b w ] -b wo where the expectation is using p ( w ) = exp b ⊺ w aw c ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc Gradient = Expected Guess - Hard Guess
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the skip-gram loss for a target word and its context word. It equals the negative log conditional probability plus the log-sum-exp normalization over the vocabulary.

blocks.text: We have (HW!)
∂ L ∂ bwo = -a wc + awc exp b ⊺ wo awc ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc ∂ L ∂ awc = -b wo + ∑ w ∈Vbw exp b ⊺ w aw c ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc
blocks.nearby_text_before: Skip-Gram Gradients So, a general term of the Skip-Gram objective looks like We have (HW!)
blocks.nearby_text_after: Notice that this is E [ b w ] -b wo where the expectation is using p ( w ) = exp b ⊺ w aw c ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc Gradient = Expected Guess - Hard Guess
blocks.formula_latex: \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_{w_o}} = -a_{w_c} + \frac{a_{w_c} \exp(b^\top w_o)}{\sum_{u\in V} \exp(b^\top u)}\qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_{w_c}} = -b_{w_o} + \sum_{w\in V} b_w \frac{\exp(b^\top w)}{\sum_{u\in V} \exp(b^\top u)}
blocks.formula_explanation: These expressions give the gradients of the skip-gram loss with respect to the output vector \(b_{w_o}\) and the center-word vector \(a_{w_c}\). Each gradient has a negative observed term plus a softmax-weighted sum over vocabulary items.

blocks.text: Skip-Gram and CBOW issues
One big problem with the Skip-Gram and the CBOW model is the (normalizing) summation ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a wc

p (w o | w c) ∼ σ (b ⊺ wo a wc) = 1 1 + exp -b ⊺ wo a wc

blocks.formula_explanation: The expression rewrites a negative-sample probability using the sigmoid function. It shows the equivalence between \(1-\sigma(x)\), \(\sigma(-x)\), and \(1/(1+\exp(x))\) for the score \(b_{w_k}^\top a_{w_c}\).

In particular, we replace

blocks.formula_latex: p(w_o\mid w_c)=\frac{\exp\, b_{w_o}^\top a_{w_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{|V|}\exp\, b_{w_j}^\top a_{w_c}}
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the conditional probability of an output word \(w_o\) given a context word \(w_c\). The numerator scores the pair \(w_o, w_c\), and the denominator normalizes over all vocabulary words.

blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the probability of a target word given a context word, using a logistic term for the positive pair and an expected product over sampled negative words. It is the negative-sampling objective used to train word embeddings.

blocks.text: We sample each word w k from the empirical distribution of the words in the corpus

blocks.formula_explanation: This is a negative-sampling loss: the first term rewards the observed word pair, and the second term penalizes sampled negative words. It is written using logistic/sigmoid form and averaged over samples from the noise distribution.

blocks.text: Word Embeddings: Negative Sampling
We replaced the original term in the objective
-log p (w o | w c) = -b ⊺ wo a wc +log |V| ∑ j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc by
blocks.nearby_text_before: Word Embeddings: Negative Sampling We replaced the original term in the objective
blocks.formula_latex: -\log p(w_o\mid w_c) = -b^\top w_o\, a_{w_c} + \log \sum_{j=1}^{|V|} \exp\! \left(b^\top w_j\, a_{w_c}\right)
blocks.formula_explanation: This is the negative log probability of a target word given a context word. The right-hand side shows a dot-product score for the observed word plus a log-sum-exp normalization over the vocabulary.

blocks.text: -b ⊺ wo a wc +log (1 + exp b ⊺ wo a wc) + K ∑ k =1, wk ∼ psample (w) log (1 + exp b ⊺ wk a wc)
blocks.nearby_text_before: Word Embeddings: Negative Sampling We replaced the original term in the objective
blocks.formula_latex: -b^\top w_o a w_c + \log(1 + \exp(b^\top w_o a w_c)) + K \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log(1 + \exp(b^\top w_k a w_c))
blocks.formula_explanation: This expression shows a negative-sampling style objective with one positive term and a sum over K negative samples. The visible pieces are logistic-style log(1+exp(·)) terms built from dot products involving word vectors.

Why drop words?
Words like 'the' don't offer much to help us understand the word 'fox'
We have enough samples with 'the' as the center words to help us understand 'the' (brown, the)

blocks.text: Why does this work?
Let's focus on CBOW and use a window of size 3
In this case, we have ( u , v , w ) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v 's B representation so the inner product we want is large
(a u + a w) ⊺ b v 2
blocks.nearby_text_before: Why does this work? Let's focus on CBOW and use a window of size 3 In this case, we have ( u , v , w ) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v 's B representation so the inner product we want is large
blocks.nearby_text_after: So I have a sentence like 'the queen ruled' and another sentence like 'the king reigned' Given this, we have that the A vectors of ( the , ruled ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of queen We also have that the A vectors of ( the , reigned ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of king
blocks.formula_explanation: The visible expression shows the dot product between the averaged context vectors \((a_u+a_w)\) and the target vector \(b_v\), with a 2 in the denominator. It is a local scoring term used to make the context representation align with the center word representation.

blocks.text: So I have a sentence like 'the queen ruled' and another sentence like 'the king reigned'
Given this, we have that the A vectors of ( the , ruled ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of queen
We also have that the A vectors of ( the , reigned ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of king
We do this many times, and get that the B vectors of 'queen' and 'king' are both enticed to be similar, since they are similar to the same words

blocks.text: Hierarchical Softmax : Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
Hierarchical Softmax is an efficient way of computing Softmax. The model uses a binary tree to represent all words in the vocabulary. The V words must be leaf units of the tree
blocks.nearby_text_before: Hierarchical Softmax: Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
blocks.nearby_text_after: For each leaf unit, there exists a unique path from the root to the unit; and this path is used to estimate the probability of the word represented by the leaf unit Main advantage: we replace the O ( |V| ) Softmax cost with O (log |V| ) The speed of this method depends on how the binary tree is built and words are associated with the leaf nodes; the paper uses a binary Huffman tree

But, what is a CNN? How can it be used?
Structure of Images
Pictures can be thought of as boxes of data
Usually, an image has a dimension H (height), W (width), C (depth, or channels) cat dog frog

blocks.text: But what is a Convolution?
In math, convolution is usually written as
(f ∗ g)(n) = M ∑ m = -M f [n -m] g [m]
blocks.nearby_text_before: But what is a Convolution? In math, convolution is usually written as
blocks.nearby_text_after: The idea above is to run some filter g across a function f and get a new function 'mixing' f and g At a high level, this is a type of moving inner product where we cross g with all the possible shifted f values
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines the convolution of two sequences at index n. It sums products of shifted samples of f and g over m from -M to M.

blocks.text: The idea above is to run some filter g across a function f and get a new function 'mixing' f and g
At a high level, this is a type of moving inner product where we cross g with all the possible shifted f values
Example of a Convolution for Images
Images are usually stored as (data) boxes of a certain width, height, and depth (channel)
Assume the depth is 1 (back-white); 'run a filter over the data over with a certain stride' 2
We move the filter across each column 1 step at a time and when we come to the end columns we go 1 row down and repeat

blocks.nearby_text_before: Images are usually stored as (data) boxes of a certain width, height, and depth (channel) Assume the depth is 1 (back-white); 'run a filter over the data over with a certain stride' 2 We move the filter across each column 1 step at a time and when we come to the end columns we go 1 row down and repeat
blocks.section_title: Example of a Convolution for Images
blocks.visual_description: A 3×3 convolution filter is applied to an input image patch and produces a single output value of 16. The example shows the elementwise multiplication and summation between the filter weights and the corresponding image region, illustrating how a sliding convolution computes each output cell.
blocks.figure_focus: Example of 2D convolution computation

blocks.text: Effects of Convolution
Note that the original data was shrunk in dimensions
Note that we moved the filter across 1 column at a time
We could have moved this 2 (or more) columns at a time, but, then we'd have a smaller result
The filter is applied to a sub-image of the exact same dimension as the filter (3 × 3), but we could have added 'padding' with this padded image to get a result of the same size as before

The colored outlines indicate the successive kernel placements that map to each output cell.

blocks.text: the bottom 48 kernels were learned on GPU 2. See Section 6.1 for details.
What about CNN models for NLP?
Given a sentence and a representation of each token in that text (one-hot, word vectors, etc.) how can we aggregate subsequences and get representations for each subsequence?
Example: 'I went to the Apple store to buy the cool new laptop.'
How can we summarize the above by using groups of length 3?
There are 10 such contiguous groups of words for the above sentence
They are: { I went to } , { went to the } , { to the Apple } . . . { cool new laptop }
Main Idea of CNN for NLP
The phrases we make, we do not care if they are grammatical
Or linguistically valid
Or cognitively plausible
What we care is trying to figure out if we can understand some sort of summary context from this information

Example
How can we represent text and do convolutions?
Use word vectors!
Example: Assume each word has dimension 5
We can use Word2Vec (or GLOVE, FastText), etc to load these word vectors
Note: I assume here that the vectors are constants for demonstration
Apply a filter of dimension 3 but note that the filter's depth (i.e. channel depth) is 5, which is the dimension of the word vectors
For NLP, these are know as 1-dimensional convolutions

The visual illustrates how text can be represented as a word-embedding matrix for convolution, with filter depth matching the embedding dimension.

blocks.text: Padding
We can also apply padding to the above, so that the resulting sentence's width is the same as the start
Dilation
Question: What does Conv1d(kernel size=2..., dilation=2) do?
Assume we have words { a , b , c , d , e , f , g , h , i , j , k } and a set of filters width 2, stride 1
A standard filter gives features made up of { a , b } , { b , c } , { c , d } , { d , e } , { e , f } , { f , g } , { g , h } , { h , i } , { i , j } , { j , k }
If we apply these type of filters to this data again, we get
features across { a , b , c } , { b , c , d } , { c , d , e } , { d , e , f } , { e , f , g } , . . .
So, with one application we we get context across words 2 at a time
With another application, we get across 3 words Etc.
Can we do this quicker?
Dilation 1 = Standard Filter
Question: What does Conv1d(kernel size=2,..., dilation=2) do?
If you have words { a , b , c , d , e , f , g , h , i , j , k } and a filter width two, stride 1, different dilations
Applying a filter with dilation 1 to the above we get features made up of

blocks.text: Max Pooling
What if you want to summarize your data across dimensions but don't want to collapse all the channels?
For each dimension of your word vectors, take the maximum coordinate as you move across the sentence 3 words at a time
In PyTorch, this can be done with
m=MaxPool1d(3, stride=1)
data=torch.rand(5, 10)

blocks.text: Big Benefit of CNN: We can apply multiple filters in parallel!
One of the big benefits of CNN is we don't need to wait for one filter to process data through another filter
On the same layer level, we can apply all the filters in parallel and then put these together to get the result

Model also has a 'hack': after back propagation, normalize all weights so that || w || 2 = s ... Do not let parameters get too large

blocks.text: CNN For Classification
The author looked at a variety of datasets with different statistics per data set MPQA
tion. c: Number of target classes. I: Average sentence length.
N: Dataset size. |V|: Vocabulary size. |Vpre|: Number of words present in the set of pre-trained word vectors. Test:
Test set size (CV means there was no standard train/test split and thus 10-fold CV was used).
CNN For Classification
Main goal was sentence classification: does the sentence have positive or negative sentiment?
Question classification: is the sentence about a person, location, number, ...
Subjective (news) or objective (opinion) language?
CNN For Classification

CNN For Classification
A nice picture of the idea
2 filters for each region size

blocks.visual_description: A sentence matrix is treated as a 7×5 input and convolved with multiple region sizes, with 2 filters per region size to produce feature maps. After activation and 1-max pooling, the pooled features are concatenated into one feature vector and passed to a softmax layer for 2-class classification.
blocks.figure_focus: CNN text classification pipeline

blocks.text: CNN For Classification
4 different models were considered
CNN-rand: Initialize randomly and let the model learn the word embeddings
CNN-static: Initialize with Word2Vec, but do not allow word vectors to be changed ( e.requires grad=False )
CNN-non-static: Initialize with Word2Vec, Embedding layer is allowed to be changed by back propagation
CNN-multichannel: Two Embeddings, both are Word2Vec but one is static another can be fine-tuned by back propagation
Aside: Word2Vec was compared with the embeddings from the paper 'NLP Almost From Scratch' (2010) which gets Word Embeddings another way - Word2Vec did much better
CNN For Classification
RAE (Socher et al., 2011)
For a very cheap model, CNNs got SOTA on 4 / 7 of the tasks considered 87.2
Note that dropout was new at this time; most other methods below would benefit from dropout 79.4 93.2 81.8 86.3
SVMs (Silva et al., 2011)
rules as features from Silva et al. (201 1).

The CNN variants include random, static, non-static, and multichannel embeddings, and the table reports their accuracy scores alongside recursive, paragraph, parser-based, Bayesian, and SVM baselines.

blocks.visual_description: Character embeddings for the words in a sentence are concatenated and fed through convolution filters of different widths to produce feature maps. Max-over-time pooling then selects the strongest activation from each filter, yielding a fixed-size representation for the input sequence.
blocks.figure_focus: CNN word vector pipeline with max-over-time pooling

blocks.text: Convolutions with kernels of size 1
The effect of 1 dimensional convolutions is to flatten all the channels together and learn channel interactions

blocks.visual_description: A 6×6×5 input volume is convolved with 1×1×5 filters using stride 1 to produce a 6×6×2 output. The example shows that a 1×1 kernel spans all input channels at each spatial location, mixing channel information without changing the spatial resolution.
blocks.figure_focus: 1×1 convolution across all channels

blocks.text: Convolutions with kernels of size 1
For NLP: we have data that is D dimensions and T sequence length
How do we reshape this data easily?
One idea is to use d a kernels of width 1 (!) and apply them to the data
This way, you now have data that is d dimensional and T in sequence length
This general idea is called down sampling
This idea is useful especially when you have residual connections and need to add h + x where x is your original data and h is the output of some block layer
This is used in the Gated CNN LM discussed above
Are convolutions still relevant? Definitely.
Christopher Rul ayerSorn( (768,), epa-le-05, elesentwine_attine-true)

blocks.text: SGD: Batch size and noisy estimates
Batches can be of size B = 1, 256, 128, etc.
The smaller the batch, the noisier the estimate
But, the smaller the batch, the more gradient updates (learning) you can do
Since we compute ∂ L B ∂θ over batches, this is a noisy estimate for the gradient of ∂ L ∂θ over the entire training set

blocks.text: Nesterov Momentum: updating quicker
The momentum term can be considered a coarse prediction of where the SGD algorithm will move next
Nesterov accelerated momentum computes the gradient at this point rather than at the current point
m t = β m t -1 +(1 -β ) ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ t -1 -ν m t -1 )

blocks.text: Issues with SGD and normalization
How do we make progress in both directions?

Gradient descent with a fixed step size has the following undesirable property: it makes large adjustments to parameters associated with large gradients (where perhaps we should be more cautious) and small adjustments to parameters associated with small gradients (where perhaps we should explore further)

When the gradient of the loss surface is much steeper in one direction than another, it is difficult to choose a learning rate that (i) makes good progress in both directions and (ii) is stable (figures 6.9a-b)
Issues with SGD
Normalized gradients a = 0.05
accomplished by normalizing the gradient magnitude and retaining only the sign.

blocks.visual_description: The four subplots compare optimization trajectories on the same loss surface using different update rules. Standard gradient descent with a small step size moves slowly and then stalls near a flat region, while a larger step size causes unstable zig-zagging. Normalized gradients produce steadier progress, and Adam follows a smoother curved path toward the minimum.

So just always use Adam?
When it came out, people got excited with Adam and its performance
After some time, people discovered that for some datasets Adam does not get to the optimal place, SGD is necessary
The initial Adam paper had a mistake in its convergence proof
How do you fix it? One idea was to run Adam and then switch to SGD after ... See the references
Generally, Adam is a good optimizer to try, but you might need to do other things like the above
4 More Tricks
Schedulers: Adjust the learning rate in a smart way
Gradient Accumulation: How to use 1 GPU like you have 4
Distributed Data Parallelism: One way to use 4 GPUs
Warm Up: Don't just go from random parameters to better all at once, do it slowly so that the model is not 'shocked'

blocks.text: Schedulers
Ideas like the above are part of a larger theory of learning rate schedulers; the goal is to help learning when it stalls
Popular schedulers in PyTorch such as ExponentialLR or ReduceLROnPlateau help change the learning rate as needed if some criterion is met
ExponentialLR: Every epoch, lower the learning rate by γ
ReduceLROnPlateau: If Val Accuracy has not gone lower for 3 iterations, lower the learning rate by γ
You can even make your own (i.e. the previous slide) ...
Gradient Accumulation
Problem: you want to use a large batch size for optimization, but you have 1 GPU
You find that for your model, you can fit just 2 training samples per GPU
What can you do?
You can, for example just use batch size 2 ... But each of these estimates are noisy
Large Batch Sizes
Key Benefits of Large Batch Sizes
Computational Efficiency:
Parallelism: Larger batches allow for better utilization of hardware resources, especially GPUs which excel at parallel matrix multiplication and other operations

Reduced Communication Overhead: Your entire data is on the CPU and as you optimize you move it to the GPU (see above). The overhead of transferring data between the CPU and GPU is relatively fixed. With larger batches, the data transfer cost per example becomes lower
Enhanced Convergence: Large batch sizes allow for better utilization of parallel processing hardware (GPUs/TPUs), reducing the time taken per epoch
Large Batch Sizes
Key Benefits of Large Batch Sizes
Improved Generalization?

Noisier Gradient Estimates: Smaller batches can introduce more noise into the gradient estimates and generalize better (See later) ; Batch Norm and learning rate warming can help mimic this even with large batch sizes
Reduced Variance: Larger batches can reduce the variance of the gradient estimates; this can lead to smoother and more consistent updates to the model parameters
Larger Steps: Larger batches allow for taking larger steps in the optimization process. This can accelerate convergence, especially in the early stages of training
One idea: start with a large batch size and then decrease it (LLAMA 3.1 used this)
Gradient Accumulation
OK, so I want to use a large batch but I'm on Colab and I have 1 GPU
One idea: Gradient Accumulation ...
Process K batches each of size B sequentially
For each batch, compute the loss gradient but do not do an update step
Instead, accumulate the K gradients by computing the gradient for a batch and sum with the previously computed gradient
Once you've processed K batches, then do a gradient step
Idea is now the gradient step is as if we've used a batch of size B ∗ K
blocks.nearby_text_before: For each batch, compute the loss gradient but do not do an update step Instead, accumulate the K gradients by computing the gradient for a batch and sum with the previously computed gradient Once you've processed K batches, then do a gradient step
blocks.text_raw: Idea is now the gradient step is as if we've used a batch of size B ∗ K
blocks.text_cleaned: Idea is now the gradient step is as if we've used a batch of size $B \ast K$

blocks.text: Gradient Accumulation
Example: I want to use a batch size of 128 but I can only fit a batch size of 32 on my GPU (data + model)
Take 4 batches, each of size 32 and feed each one sequentially through the model and then get a gradient on each batch

blocks.text: What if you have multiple GPUs?
What would I do if I had 4 GPUs and each could fit the model and a batch of maximum size 32?
The idea now is something called Distributed Data Parallelism
Idea: make a copy of each model, one on each GPU
For a batch of size 128, split it into 4 chunks of size 32, and feed a batch of size 32 to each GPU
Compute the gradient of the model on each GPU - note the model is assumed to be the same on each GPU
Once we have the 4 computed gradients, accumulate them as before to get a gradient which effectively is of size 128
Put this gradient onto each GPU
Apply the same gradient step to each model on each GPU
Now, as before, each GPU has the same model but we've done a gradient step of size 128 effectively and not 32 ....
Distributed Data Parallelism is a library in PyTorch you can leveredge
So do you always want to use a large batch?
Rule of thumb: smaller batch sizes generalize better
Large Batch sizes converge to sharp minima where while small batches converge to flat minima

If small perturbations the optima vector cause a lot of data misclassification, the minimizer is sharp; a small movement away from the optimal parameters causes a large increase in the loss function. In contrast, flat minima have accuracy that remains nearly constant under small parameter perturbations

In contrast, flat minima have accuracy that remains nearly constant under small parameter perturbations

blocks.visual_description: The plot compares training and testing loss landscapes around two minima. A broad flat minimum is shown where the test function stays close to the training function, while a narrow sharp minimum shows the test function rising more strongly under small parameter changes. The labeled curves illustrate the generalization idea that flat minima are more robust than sharp minima.
blocks.figure_focus: Flat versus sharp minima and generalization

blocks.text: So what do I do? Use small batch size?
Generally speaking, you need to try out experiments with the batch size and see what happens
There is no clean recipe always
For example, people use learning rate warm up to get large batch training to still generalize well
Notice both Batch Size and Ir are increasing by 2 every time

blocks.nearby_text_before: Generally speaking, you need to try out experiments with the batch size and see what happens There is no clean recipe always
blocks.section_title: So what do I do? Use small batch size?
blocks.visual_description: Validation loss is plotted against epochs for four batch sizes, with learning rates adjusted for each setting. All curves drop sharply at the beginning, reach a similar low region, and then gradually rise, suggesting comparable training behavior across batch sizes under tuned learning rates.
blocks.figure_focus: Batch size comparison with adjusted learning rates

blocks.text: Warm up?
Idea is, we want to use a learning rate of 0 . 1
However, we don't want this all at once
Instead, increase the learning rate gradually from 0 to 0.1 in a few optimization epochs
Once we get to 0.1, then dampen it
Analytic Example: lr = α min { 1 √ t , t √ 10 3 }

blocks.text: Anything else about small batch size?
From GoodFellow's Deep Learning (2016):
Small batches can offer a regularizing effect (Wilson and Martinez, 2003), perhaps due to the noise they add to the learning process
Generalization error is often best for a batch size of 1
Training with such a small batch size might require a small learning rate to maintain stability because of the high variance in the estimate of the gradient
The total runtime can be very high as a result of the need to make more steps, both because of the reduced learning rate and because it takes more steps to observe the entire training set

blocks.text: BatchNorm
We optimize a Network over batches and batches can change
A batch might change distribution, so for example x feeding into σ might be all over the place
The loss will be very bouncy
Idea: Normalize the data before feeding into the nonlinearity!
BatchNorm
For each batch B , compute µ B and σ 2 B which are the mean and variance of the different components of x in the batch B (these are vectors, of the same dimension as x )
blocks.nearby_text_after: For example, if B = { x 1,..., x m } and each x i ∈ R d, then so are µ B and σ 2 B but each component of these vectors is the mean / variance of the respective components of all batch vectors x b, i.e. ( µ B )( j ) = ∑ m i =1 xi ( j ) m Before we feed into σ, normalize each vector x ∈ B so that we use ˆ x = x -µ B √ σ 2 B + ϵ Now, feed this normalized ˆ x into σ: less chance we'll get extremes!

blocks.text: BatchNorm: Training and Inference
At training time, we compute µ B and σ 2 B for each batch and use these statistics in gradient computations
At inference time, we can recompute these statistics over the entire training set and use these as normalizations
Or, we can use some sort of running estimate (momentum!)
For example, if we process mini-batches B 1 , B 2 , . . . , B T in sequence, we can define a mean estimate as µ B = µ B 1 , then µ B = αµ B 2 +(1 -α ) µ B etc

LayerNorm
For NLP applications, usually we have batches of sentences and each sentence has different tokens and different lengths
Each token has an embedding
We can't really take a mean across all embeddings in the batch, since different embeddings are in different sentences
We'd lose context if we did this (imagine we have to pad short sentences - those 0's would affect the means)
For NLP, a batch is of dimension B × T × d

Drop Out
The aim of dropout is to make sure the model does not overtrain too much
At a high level, for each batch we zero out a random proportion (20 %, 30 %) of the neurons
This effectively zeros out the parameters for these neurons
For a certain weight W , we effectively use D ∗ W where D is a Bernoulli random variable that is 1/0 with probability 1 -p and p
blocks.nearby_text_before: The aim of dropout is to make sure the model does not overtrain too much At a high level, for each batch we zero out a random proportion (20 %, 30 %) of the neurons This effectively zeros out the parameters for these neurons
blocks.text_raw: For a certain weight W , we effectively use D ∗ W where D is a Bernoulli random variable that is 1/0 with probability 1 -p and p
blocks.text_cleaned: For a certain weight W, we effectively use D * W where D is a Bernoulli random variable that is 1/0 with probability 1-p and p

blocks.text: Drop Out
You can think of this method kind of like ensembling: on each batch, you are training a slightly different model that is weakened vs the full model
training with a slightly different network each time.

blocks.nearby_text_before: You can think of this method kind of like ensembling: on each batch, you are training a slightly different model that is weakened vs the full model training with a slightly different network each time.
blocks.visual_description: A neural network is shown in four states: the full network and several versions where some hidden units are randomly omitted or disabled. The shaded gray nodes indicate dropped units, illustrating how different sub-networks are trained while the visible connections still map inputs x1, x2, x3 to output y.
blocks.figure_focus: Dropout as training subnetworks by random unit removal

blocks.text: Drop Out
You can also think dropout as making the model learn as much as it can in this weakened state: do the best with what you have, don't get too comfortable using one set of nodes
This makes the network less dependent on any given hidden unit
We also encourage the weights to have smaller magnitudes so that the change in the function due to the presence or absence of the hidden unit is reduced

Finally, we will see how this is done with RNNs and we will find out how to 'generate' from an Language Model a sentence
Language Models: Why?
Language models in some sense allow us to understand how words follow each other and have many applications
Given a question, tell me the answer (identify the answer in some context)
Given the start of a sentence, tell me the next few words (generate text)
From a language model, we might extract contextual word representations (vectors) and apply them to other things - we'll see this later
Etc.
The vocabulary set V
First, we will define V to be the set of all words in the language
For example, this can be V = { the, dog, laughs, saw, barks, cat, . . . }
Assume V can be quite large
Assume V is finite
Given this corpus, we'd like to estimate the parameters of a Language Model
A sentence is a sequence of words

blocks.formula_explanation: This is a sequence of tokens written as a product-like string: $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T-1}$. It represents the word positions in a sentence or corpus example up to time $T-1$.

blocks.text: Language Models
We assume that x T is a special symbol STOP which is not in V
Depending on the model, we also have a symbol START which we will use; this is also not in V
This will typically be for x 0 or x -1 as we will see
So: all sentences begin with START and end in STOP
A sentence ( x 1 x 2 . . . x T -1 ) can then have the padded form ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) or ( x -2 , x -1 , x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) Etc.
blocks.nearby_text_before: Depending on the model, we also have a symbol START which we will use; this is also not in V This will typically be for x 0 or x -1 as we will see So: all sentences begin with START and end in STOP
blocks.nearby_text_after: Some example sentences are as follows START the dog barks STOP START the STOP START STOP
blocks.text_raw: A sentence ( x 1 x 2 . . . x T -1 ) can then have the padded form ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) or ( x -2 , x -1 , x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) Etc.
blocks.text_cleaned: A sentence \((x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T-1})\) can then have the padded form \((x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_T)\) or \((x_{-2}, x_{-1}, x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_T)\) Etc.

blocks.text: Some example sentences are as follows
START the dog barks STOP
START the STOP START STOP
START START cat cat cat STOP
START the cat saw the dog STOP
Notice: Some sentences make more sense than others! Good sentences should have a higher 'score'

Markov Language Models
Markov Language Models are some of the oldest models whose goal is to do better than the above
To start, let is look at p ( x 0 , . . . , x T ) again and apply Bayes' rule in its 'chain rule' way

Perplexity: How to measure Language Model Goodness
How do we know if a Language Model is good?
Ideally, it should give a high probability to a good sentence, and a low probability to a bad sentence
We always minimize the Loss L = -∑ N i =1 log p ( x ( i ) ) N , but in NLP we also use another metric called the Perplexity to quantify model goodness

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
A picture of a RNN might look like this:

blocks.visual_description: A repeating module of a standard recurrent neural network is shown across time steps, with input vectors x_{t-1}, x_t, x_{t+1} feeding into the same shared neural network block A and producing hidden states h_{t-1}, h_t, h_{t+1}. The middle step explicitly includes a tanh nonlinearity, illustrating how the recurrent update combines the current input and previous state into the next hidden state.
blocks.figure_focus: Standard RNN repeating module recursion

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks: Computational Graph
RNN: Computational Graph: Many to Many

blocks.visual_description: An RNN unrolled over time maps inputs x1, x2, x3, ..., xT into hidden states h1, h2, h3, ..., hT through a repeated transition function f_W with shared weights W. Each time step produces an output y1, y2, y3, ..., yT with its own loss L1, L2, L3, ..., LT, and these losses contribute to a total objective L.
blocks.figure_focus: RNN many-to-many computational graph

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
As the data gets longer, an RNN can no longer propagate dependencies between things far in the future and elements earlier on

blocks.visual_description: A recurrent neural network is shown as a repeated cell A unrolled across time, with inputs x0, x1, x2, x3, x4 producing hidden states h0 through h4. A second unrolled sequence below highlights the same recurrence extending to later time steps, with x_t and x_{t+1} mapped to h_t, h_{t+1}, and h_{t+2}, emphasizing parameter sharing and temporal dependency.
blocks.figure_focus: RNN unrolling across time steps

blocks.text: If a is small, we have that the term measuring the dependency between the current time step and the one in the past is less and less
This is called the vanishing gradient problem
If a is too large, we'll have an infinite gradient, another problem
This is called the exploding gradient problem

blocks.text: If h t is going to predict y t , we clearly see old information 'vanishes' if a is small
In some sense this illustrates the problem
Recurrent Neural Networks: Solving Gradient Issues
The exploding gradient problem can be solved by gradient clipping
The vanishing gradient problem might be solved by other ways
Residual connections
Xavier Initialization (some sort of smart initialization)
Momentum based optimizers (Adam)
None of the above are principled, they 'might' work
In practice, vanilla RNNs do not perform as good as they theoretically can due to the vanishing gradient problem
More complicated (LSTM, Transformers, Attention) models are used
Recurrent Neural Networks: Truncated BP
Doing back propagation in RNN models can be expensive since you need to go all the way back to t = 0
We generally have
∂ L t ∂ a = t ∑ s =1 ∂ L t ∂ ˆ y t ∂ ˆ y t ∂ h t ∂ h t ∂ h s ∂ h s ∂ a

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks: Bidirectional RNN
You can also have models which have context on both sides
Imagine the sentiment analysis of 'The great movie was actually really just expensive and bad.'
You'd like to know that 'great' has a bit of irony in it and it contrasts with 'expensive and bad'
Sentiment analysis is one example where a bidirectional RNN might be a good idea: each word has the best context of those in the sentence!

blocks.nearby_text_before: Imagine the sentiment analysis of 'The great movie was actually really just expensive and bad.' You'd like to know that 'great' has a bit of irony in it and it contrasts with 'expensive and bad' Sentiment analysis is one example where a bidirectional RNN might be a good idea: each word has the best context of those in the sentence!
blocks.visual_description: A recurrent sequence model is shown in two directions: the top chain processes inputs x0, x1, x2, ... , xi forward through repeated hidden states A, producing a final state s_i. The bottom chain processes the same inputs with a second recurrent pathway A' and outputs y0, y1, y2, ... , yi, while also carrying states from both left and right directions, illustrating how context from the whole sequence can influence each position.
blocks.figure_focus: Bidirectional RNN with forward and backward context

blocks.text: Recurrent Neural Networks: Bidirectional RNN
When you are generating text, you can't use a truly bidirectional RNN (given our architecture) since then you are cheating
For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally
Imagine 'He painted the wall with a brush, not a spray can.'

blocks.text: Outline
Language Modeling
Recurrent Neural Networks
LSTM
Decoding
LSTM
RNN suffer from vanishing gradient problems
Because of this, people have tried to come up with variants that resolve this issue
LSTM (1997 !) are one of the most popular variants
Empirically, an LSTM has been shown to have a memory of about 100 time steps, whereas a RNN has about just 7
LSTM
Dynamics of the LSTM are as follows
We still have data x = ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) but now we have a more complicated interaction

blocks.nearby_text_after: Specifically, each time step not only has h t, but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward Where the state is And the hidden state is

blocks.text_cleaned: We still have data $x = (x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_T)$ but now we have a more complicated interaction

blocks.text: Specifically, each time step not only has h t , but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward
We have
(f t, i t, o t, g t) = Whht -1 + Wxxt + b
blocks.nearby_text_before: Dynamics of the LSTM are as follows We still have data x = ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) but now we have a more complicated interaction Specifically, each time step not only has h t , but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward
blocks.nearby_text_after: Where the state is And the hidden state is
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula computes a joint affine transformation from the previous hidden state and current input. Its output is a 4-tuple of gate pre-activations (f_t, i_t, o_t, g_t) used in the LSTM.

blocks.text: Where the state is
c t = σ (f t) ⊙ c t -1 + σ (i t) ⊙ tanh (g t)
blocks.nearby_text_before: We still have data x = ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) but now we have a more complicated interaction Specifically, each time step not only has h t , but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward Where the state is
blocks.nearby_text_after: And the hidden state is
blocks.formula_latex: c t = \sigma(f t) ⊙ c t -1 + \sigma(i t) ⊙ tanh(g t)
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula updates the LSTM cell state by combining the previous cell state with a new candidate state. The forget gate and input gate, passed through sigmoid functions, control how much old memory is kept and how much new information is added.

blocks.text: And the hidden state is
h t = σ (o t) ⊙ tanh (c t)
blocks.nearby_text_before: Specifically, each time step not only has h t , but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward Where the state is And the hidden state is
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula computes the hidden state $h_t$ by taking the elementwise product of the output gate $\sigma(o_t)$ and the cell state transformed by $\tanh(c_t)$. It shows how the LSTM exposes part of its cell content as the hidden output at time $t$.

blocks.text: LSTM
The key new feature here is
c t = σ (f t) ⊙ c t -1 + σ (i t) ⊙ tanh (g t)
blocks.nearby_text_before: The key new feature here is
blocks.nearby_text_after: σ ( f t ) is the forget gate σ ( i t ) is the update gate This cell state is a combination of old information and new information: the sole purpose of this state is to help the network not forget!
blocks.formula_latex: c t = \sigma(f t) ⊙ c t -1 + \sigma(i t) ⊙ tanh(g t)
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula updates the cell state by mixing the previous state with a new candidate value. The forget gate scales old information, and the input gate scales the new content before they are added together.

blocks.text: σ ( f t ) is the forget gate

blocks.section_title: Here is a nice picture of what is going on in an LSTM
blocks.visual_description: The figure shows an LSTM unrolled across time, with the central cell exposing four interacting components that process the current input x_t and prior hidden state to produce h_t. It illustrates the flow of information through gate operations, tanh nonlinearity, and elementwise multiplication/addition, with neighboring time steps feeding h_{t-1} and h_{t+1}.

blocks.text: LSTM: Idea
The big idea of LSTM networks is that the added c t allows the networks to remember things
Put another way, if we write out gradients, there is a greater chance of long term dependencies
Why?
Recall, terms of the type ∂ ht ∂ ht -k are critical to gradient estimates for the RNN, let's focus on them since they are a (critical) part of all gradients

blocks.text: LSTMs now
Are LSTMs dead now?
Sort of, but there are some ideas which may bring them back
Why are they dead?
Main problem: parallelism
If I give you a batch of sentences, to compute this batch you need to go one by one though each word in each sentence to get the hidden states
You can parallelize across difference sentences, but not within each sentence
GPU cannot be used as well as possible
Note: People have been trying, see references
Outline
Language Modeling
Recurrent Neural Networks
LSTM
Decoding
Decoding: Example
Once we train a model, we have the problem of decoding: How do we generate text using the model?
Usually, we start with a context like 'The man walks' or a special start token ' START ' and begin decoding with the model
One very simple variant: greedy decoding
This is one of the oldest and easiest heuristics
Decoding: Greedy Search Example
Basic idea: Always pick the highest probability next word and use that as your next word
Once picked, add the word to your context and repeat until some terminal condition happens
Big idea is that, for example, we have
p ( The , man , walks , y ) = p ( y | The , man , walks ) p ( The , man , walks )

Also, human generated text is not generated this way: you don't always pick the word with the next highest probability to generate the things you say Sometimes, you make a lower probability choice but in the long run this leads to higher probability choices overall
blocks.section_title: Decoding: Greedy is not Optimal, Random might be too 'random'
blocks.formula_explanation: This expression takes the maximum of the sequence probability p(y_1,y_2,\ldots,y_T) over all choices of y_1 through y_T. It represents selecting the most probable sequence.

blocks.text: Greedy, generally, will not give the optimal solution - we keep just one choice!
Also, human generated text is not generated this way: you don't always pick the word with the next highest probability to generate the things you say
Sometimes, you make a lower probability choice but in the long run this leads to higher probability choices overall
Think of generating this word by word: 'The Himalayan Mountains are very nice'
Another idea: Beam Search
Decoding: Beam Search
Instead of picking the next highest probability word to add to your context, store a beam of size k as your set of candidates
Example: Assume k = 2 and your context is again ' The man walks '

Attention
NMT
Sometimes we want to go from one input sequence to another output sequence
These are known as Seq2Seq problems
Some examples:
Machine Translation: Go from one language to another language
Summarization: Go from a large piece of text to a smaller piece of text
Chatbots: Generate a piece of text which is an answer to some input question
Code Generation: Words to Python code
Named Entity Recognition: Sentence to Tags
Parsing: Sentence to output parse
We'll focus on Translation here
Neural MT vs Statistical MT
It would be good to know how this was done before by looking at SMT = Statistical Machine Translation
Suppose we want to translate from x (French) to y (English)
Let x have length T x and y have length T y
From 1990 - 2010, people focused on modeling p ( y | x )

blocks.text: SMT
For such a model like IBM 2, you'd want to estimate q w and q d which handle the probability of a word generating another word and a position generating another position
-N ∑ i =1 log p (x (i) | y (i)) = -N ∑ i =1 log ∑ a (i) p (x (i), a (i) | y (i)) = -N ∑ i =1 log ∑ a (i) T (i) x ∏ t =1 qw (x (i) t | y (i) a (i) t) q d (a (i) t | t)
blocks.nearby_text_before: For such a model like IBM 2, you'd want to estimate q w and q d which handle the probability of a word generating another word and a position generating another position
blocks.nearby_text_after: As above, you'd write out the likelihood over data { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1 and estimate q w and q d via Expectation Maximization We hope good alignment quality (see next slide) correlated with a low Loss Once you have these parameters estimated, you can recover the alignments via
blocks.formula_latex: -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p\bigl(x^{(i)} \mid y^{(i)}\bigr) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sum_{a^{(i)}} p\bigl(x^{(i)}, a^{(i)} \mid y^{(i)}\bigr) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sum_{a^{(i)}} T_x^{(i)} \prod_{t=1}^{T_x^{(i)}} q_w\bigl(x_t^{(i)} \mid y_{a_t^{(i)}}^{(i)}\bigr)\, q_d\bigl(a_t^{(i)} \mid t\bigr)
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula writes the negative log-likelihood of the observed sentence pairs, then rewrites it by summing over latent alignments. The last form expands the joint probability into word-translation and distortion factors across positions.

blocks.formula_latex: a = argmax a 1, \ldots, aTx Tx \prod_{t=1} q w(x t | y at) q d(a t | t)
blocks.formula_explanation: This expression selects the alignment sequence a that maximizes a product over positions t from 1 to T_x. The factors are a word translation term q_w(x_t\mid y_{a_t}) and a distortion/alignment prior q_d(a_t\mid t).

blocks.text: SMT
Once you get the alignment from your model, you can measure its quality vs some human generated alignment and recover a metric like Alignment Error Rate (AER)
AER was loosely negatively correlated with translation quality, so people searched for models with better and better AER
Lower AER → Better Phrases → Better Translation Models
Translation models were complicated, and so were alignment models
Note: to get argmax y p ( x | y ) p ( y ) you can use Beam Search, or another heuristic; Beam Search is a general decoding method

blocks.text: SMT was a huge research field
The best systems were extremely complex
There were lots of important details that we skip
Systems had many separately designed subcomponents
Lots of feature engineering
You needed to do particular feature design to capture various language effects
SMT required compiling and maintaining extra resources
First, alignments needed to be identified and then phrases needed to be constructed
For example, we needed to keep tables of equivalent phrases
Lots of human effort to maintain
Lots of repeated effort for each language pair!
NMT
Figure 1: Our model reads an input sentence "ABC" and produces "WXYZ" as the output sentence. The
What is Neural Machine Translation?
At a high level, NMT is doing machine translation but using a single network
Let's spell out the picture above a bit more
Here, x is the input, y is the output; T x , T y are the number of tokens in each
NMT was a small effort in 2014 - it blew up!

blocks.text: 2 Models: An encoder and a decoder
Encoder and decoder were usually 2 separate RNN models (no parameter sharing)
Encoder RNN can have a different structure than the decoder RNN (encoder = LSTM, decoder = special GRU (?))
The representation of the input would usually be the final encoder hidden states h Tx
h Tx = k 0 , the final encoder hidden layer states are the initial decoder hidden layer states

NMT: Layers
To get the best performance, we can use a (bidirectional) stacked LSTM for the encoder
The decoder RNN is autoregressive: it can't look into the future (it's a sort of language model!)
We can use a stacked (one directional) LSTM for the decoder
The idea is that lower layers compute general features, while higher layers compute more complex interactions: both are crucial
Seems like 2-4 layers is about the sweet spot in the number of layers needed
2 layers better than 1
3 layers a little better than 2
The more layers you have, the more you'll need connections, tricks, etc
NMT vs SMT - Advantages
Compared to SMT, NMT has many advantages
Better performance
More fluent
Better use of context
Better use of phrase similarities
1 Neural Network, not 12 models in a disjoint 'pipeline'
Much less human engineering effort
NMT vs SMT - Disadvantages
Disadvantages of NMT?
NMT is less interpretable and hard to debug
NMT is difficult to control: we can't really put in rules and and get the result we want
BLEU - How do we say a translation is 'good'?
How do we measure the quality of a translation?
Source: Le chat est sur le tapis
MT System Output: the the the the the the the
Reference 1: The cat is on the mat
Reference 2: There is a cat on the mat
How do we know this is a bad translation?
One idea is to measure the precision of the translation
For a general MT output we can get the precision by
Getting all the words (tokens) in the generated translation
Counting if each word is or is not in the reference translations
Dividing by the number of words in the generated translation
For the example above we have 1 word 7 times
'the' is seen in at least one of the reference sentences

BLEU
So, clipping solved one problem
But, what if we have
Translation 1: The cat is on the mat
Translation 2 (Yoda): Cat on the mat it is
Clearly, Translation 1 is better but we would not pick this up if we just use tokens w that are unigrams (words)
To this end, we can use bigrams, trigrams, and 4-grams to capture word order
For example, for Translation 1, we have bigrams 'the cat', 'cat is', 'is on', 'on the', 'the mat'
We can then do the same exact thing as before, but this time use w as a bigram instead
BLEU
The precisions we calculate for 1, 2, 3 and 4 grams are p i
Then, BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is
BLEU = (p 1 p 2 p 3 p 4) 1 / 4

blocks.text: Hard to get the gist
The ranges of BLEU indicate empirically how good the translation is
There might be better metrics, for example 'GLEU', ''RIBES', 'BEER', etc
BLEU is still the most standard metric used today

blocks.visual_description: A table maps BLEU score ranges to qualitative translation quality interpretations. Scores below 10 are described as almost useless, 10–19 as hard to get the gist, 20–29 as understandable but with significant grammatical errors, and higher ranges progress to high quality and even better than human translation.
blocks.figure_focus: BLEU score interpretation ranges

blocks.text: BLEU - More on Positives and Negatives
Positives
It is quick to calculate and corresponds with the way a human would evaluate the same text
It is used very widely, which makes it easier to compare your results with other work
Importantly, it is language-independent making it straightforward to apply to your NLP models
It can be used when you have more than one ground truth sentence
Negatives

It does not consider the meaning of words. It is perfectly acceptable to a human to use a different word with the same meaning eg. Use 'watchman' instead of 'guard'. But BLEU Score considers that an incorrect word
It looks only for exact word matches. Sometimes a variant of the same word can be used eg. 'rain' and 'raining', but BLEU Score counts that as an error

It ignores the importance of words. With BLEU Score an incorrect word like 'to' or 'an' that is less relevant to the sentence is penalized just as heavily as a word that contributes significantly to the meaning of the sentence

It does not consider the order of words eg. The sentence 'The guard arrived late because of the rain' and 'The rain arrived late because of the guard' would get the same (unigram) BLEU Score even though the latter is quite different
BLEU and NMT
In 2014, there was a fringe attempt to use NMT
By 2016, NMT took over - Google switched to NMT
SMT essentially died out as its performance had somewhat plateaued 10

blocks.nearby_text_before: In 2014, there was a fringe attempt to use NMT By 2016, NMT took over - Google switched to NMT
blocks.visual_description: Bar chart of BLEU scores on newstest2013 for EN→DE across years 2013 to 2016. The scores rise gradually from roughly 20 to the mid-20s, with a notable jump in 2016 reaching 24.7, indicating improved neural machine translation performance over time.
blocks.figure_focus: BLEU improvement for NMT over 2013–2016

blocks.text: NMT Issues
Currently, problems in NMT focus on still to be solved critical problems
Low resource languages (what do we do if we have no data?)
Domain mismatch between train and test data
Out of vocabulary words
Bias pick up (gender neutral sentences become gender specific)
Maintaining context over longer pairs (we'll see a valuable tool for this next)
Try it: there are lots of examples where Google Translate breaks down for specific languages

blocks.text: Attention: Motivation
Imagine we have a sentence x = 'I went to the flower market to buy some flowers'
Imagine the translation is y = 'Ich ging zum Blumenmarkt, um Blumen zu kaufen '
If we compress everything into h Tx , the resulting vector needs to contain
Information about the subject ('I')
Information about the verbs ('buy' and 'went')
Information about the objects ('flowers' and 'flower market')
Interaction of the subjects, verbs, and objects with each other in the sentence
This is a lot of information!
Attention
Typically the decoder's hidden state is a function of the input and the previous time step's hidden step,
k t = f (y t, k t -1)

blocks.text: Attention: Benefits
Attention improves NMT performance
It is useful to allow the decoder to focus on certain parts of the source
Attention solves the bottleneck problem
Attention allows the decoder to look directly at the source; we bypass the bottleneck h Tx
Attention helps with vanishing gradient problems
We have provided the model with shortcuts to distant states
Attention offers some interpretability
By inspecting the attention distribution, we can see what the decoder was focusing on

Attention: Local Variants
One problem with the Global attention above is that perhaps we will have too many computations for very long source sentences (documents)
We can define 'Local' attention which aims to focus on a specific subset of source sentence words
To get this working, we can set D to a constant and then define for each target position t an index p t in the source sentence
To compute the context vector c t , we just focus on an interval [ p t -D , p t + D ] of source words

blocks.text: Big Ideas Before the Transformer
There were some big ideas right before the Transformer; we'll look a few just so you understand the high level idea
Main Benefit: Large Language Models understand general patterns and we can exploit these generalities on many problems
Three main results
ELMO + Fine Tuning
Small: 14 M Parameters
Medium: 28 M Parameters
Large: 94 M Parameters
Idea: Bigger is better
ULM-Fit + Fine Tuning
216 M Parameters
Idea: Bigger is better
Attention LSTMs
Idea: Bring information from the past directly to a timestep in an RNN
Idea 1: ELMo
ELMo: Embeddings from Language Models
Goal: Construct a mechanism for contextual embeddings
I went to the river bank to watch the fish
I went to the bank to get a check
'Bank' above has very different meanings, so we want a model which allows us to differentiate LSTM Layer #1|
Idea: Train a general LSTM to model language, then use the output of that LSTM to get word embeddings for each word or for the entire sentence

blocks.nearby_text_before: I went to the bank to get a check 'Bank' above has very different meanings, so we want a model which allows us to differentiate LSTM Layer #1| Idea: Train a general LSTM to model language, then use the output of that LSTM to get word embeddings for each word or for the entire sentence
blocks.visual_description: A stacked bidirectional language model is shown with an embedding layer feeding LSTM layer #1 and LSTM layer #2, followed by an FFNN + softmax output over possible English words. The example illustrates contextual prediction for the phrase fragment "Let's stick to" and shows that the same word can map to different output probabilities depending on surrounding context. The nearby text about the word "bank" and the section title indicate this is used to derive contextual word embeddings in ELMo.
blocks.figure_focus: ELMo contextual embeddings from stacked LSTMs

blocks.text: ELMo
To get bidirectional context but nor cheat, use two LSTMs one going left to right and another going right to left; fuse the results ... We have weak bidirectionality
The LSTM objective is to maximize
T ∑ k =1 [log p (x t | x 1,..., x t -1, - → Θ) + log p (x t | x t +1,..., x T, ← -Θ)]
blocks.nearby_text_before: To get bidirectional context but nor cheat, use two LSTMs one going left to right and another going right to left; fuse the results ... We have weak bidirectionality The LSTM objective is to maximize
blocks.nearby_text_after: The input data and output softmax weights are shared by both models
blocks.formula_latex: T \sum_{k=1} [log p(x t | x 1, \ldots, x t -1, - → Θ) + log p(x t | x t +1, \ldots, x T, ← -Θ)]
blocks.formula_explanation: This expression adds the log probabilities of predicting the current token from left context and from right context. It represents the training objective for combining forward and backward language models.

blocks.text: The input data and output softmax weights are shared by both models

blocks.visual_description: A forward language model is shown built from two stacked LSTM layers operating on token embeddings for the sequence "Let's stick to". Each time step feeds upward through the LSTM layers, illustrating how hidden states are propagated to predict language context from left to right. The figure emphasizes the internal recurrence of an LSTM-based language model rather than a complete bidirectional ELMo representation.
blocks.figure_focus: Forward LSTM language model over token sequence

blocks.nearby_text_before: To get bidirectional context but nor cheat, use two LSTMs one going left to right and another going right to left; fuse the results... blocks.visual_description: A backward language model is illustrated with token predictions flowing from right to left, showing how each hidden state uses future context to predict the current word. The example sequence at the bottom reads "Let's stick to" with arrows pointing backward through the tokens, emphasizing reverse-direction context accumulation.

blocks.visual_description: The figure contrasts two training stages for BERT: first semi-supervised pretraining on large unlabeled text such as books and Wikipedia with a masked-word prediction objective, then supervised fine-tuning on a labeled task using a classifier. The second stage shows the pretrained model being adapted to spam detection with example email inputs and class outputs.
blocks.figure_focus: BERT pretraining followed by task-specific fine-tuning

blocks.text: Idea 2: ULM-Fit, Language Models and Text Classification
Another method that arose around 2018 was ULM-Fit: Universal Language Models Fine Tuning
Goal here was to do Text Classification but not from scratch; they gave their model a big head start by generic training
High level idea
Train a general Language Model to predict the next token
Fine-tune the language model to predict the corpus you are now interested in but be careful how you apply gradient steps to each layer
Add a classifier 'head' on top and train the model to predict the specific class for the data (Spam/No Spam, { 1, 2, 3, 4 } etc) you are interested in
ULM-Fit: Step by Step
Nice visual of the main 3 steps for ULM-Fit
Step 1: General LM: Get generic features
Step 2: Specific LM: learning rate depends on layer, domain specific data Layer 1 Layer 1 layer The best scene ever (c) Classifier fine-tuning
(a) LM pre-training
Step 3: Classifier Head: gradual unfreezing and fine-tuning of the 'head' 'Discr', and STLR to
preserve low-level representations and adapt high-level ones (shaded: unfreezing stages; black: frozen).

blocks.nearby_text_before: Nice visual of the main 3 steps for ULM-Fit Step 1: General LM: Get generic features Step 2: Specific LM: learning rate depends on layer, domain specific data Layer 1 Layer 1 layer The best scene ever (c) Classifier fine-tuning
blocks.visual_description: A three-stage transfer-learning pipeline for ULMFiT is shown with a stacked neural language model and a classifier head. The stages are LM pre-training on general text, LM fine-tuning on target-domain text, and classifier fine-tuning where the pretrained layers are adapted with discriminative learning rates and the top classifier is trained for the task.
blocks.figure_focus: ULMFiT three-step fine-tuning pipeline

blocks.text: Idea 3: LSTM models and Attention
Eventually, people started using LSTM models for translation
Given 'A, B, C, D', generate 'X Y Z'
Idea: Encoder LSTM encodes 'A, B, C, D', decoder LSTM generates 'X Y Z' as if it were a language model
Final hidden layer of the encoder LSTM is the initial hidden layer of the decoder LSTM
Problem: The model is asked too much: that final vector needs to have all the information about input 'A B C D'

blocks.nearby_text_before: Eventually, people started using LSTM models for translation Given 'A, B, C, D', generate 'X Y Z' Idea: Encoder LSTM encodes 'A, B, C, D', decoder LSTM generates 'X Y Z' as if it were a language model
blocks.visual_description: A neural machine translation architecture is shown as a stacked recurrent encoder-decoder: the source sequence A, B, C, D is encoded by a sequence of LSTM-like states, then the decoder generates the target sequence X, Y, Z step by step. The diagram marks <eos> tokens at the end of both source and target sequences to indicate sentence boundaries.
blocks.figure_focus: Stacked encoder-decoder translation with LSTM

blocks.text: Attention: Global Variants
ment weight vector a based on the current target state hy and all source states hs. A global context
vector ct is then computed as the weighted aver- age, according to at, over all the source states.

blocks.nearby_text_before: ment weight vector a based on the current target state hy and all source states hs. A global context vector ct is then computed as the weighted aver- age, according to at, over all the source states.
blocks.visual_description: An attention layer computes a context vector c_t as a weighted combination of all source states, with the weights labeled a_t as global alignment weights. The target state h_t and output y_t are produced using this context, while the source sequence states h_s feed into the weighting mechanism.
blocks.figure_focus: Global attention with context vector and alignment weights

blocks.text: Outline
Before the Transformer
The Transformer
GPT1
The Transformer
Up until 2017 researchers were mainly interested in LSTM / RNN models and variants of these models
Basically, you take a task and then use the building blocks you have to get a model to work well on it
Example 1 which we saw: ELMo
Another Example: BiDAF ... Lots of LSTMs, different types of Attention
Another Example like ELMo: COVE ... Lots of LSTMs and Attention mechanisms
At a high level, they used LSTM models, Attention, and insights so that a model can be fit well to the task
The Transformer: The Model
The Transformer represents a massive paradigm shift in NLP, where a new model architecture took over
Almost all of the models currently 'hot' (Alpaca, ChatGPT, GPT4, Bard) are Transformer-based models

blocks.nearby_text_before: The Transformer represents a massive paradigm shift in NLP, where a new model architecture took over
blocks.visual_description: The figure shows the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with input embeddings and positional encoding feeding stacked encoder blocks, and shifted-right output embeddings feeding stacked decoder blocks. The decoder uses masked multi-head attention over the target sequence, then attends to encoder outputs, followed by feed-forward layers, Add & Norm steps, and a final linear layer with softmax to produce output probabilities.
blocks.figure_focus: Transformer encoder-decoder architecture

blocks.text: The Transformer: Why?
What's wrong with RNN models given what we saw before?
Main issue: people started observing that you can train large models and then fine tune them to different tasks
Think ULM-Fit: train an LSTM and then fine-tune
Or ELMo: train an LSTM and then for each token get a mixture of the hidden layer representations,
ElMo (x t) = γ task L ∑ l =0 s task lhlt,

blocks.text: Problem: Generally, bigger models were seen to do better, but RNNs can't be parallelized!
Why? For each element in a batch, you need to traverse its full length T sequentially, you cannot parallelize
The Transformer: We Want Parallelism
Contrast this with a CNN: if we run a filter over the data, we can parallelize the application of that filter to different chunks of the data
Moreover, we can also parallelize across filters
The transformer replaced the RNN/LSTM idea with an Attention based substitute that allows for parallelism
The effective batch size of a Transformer/CNN is the number of words
Contrast this with an LSTM: effective batch size is the number of sequences

blocks.text: What is Self-Attention?
Big idea of the Transformer: Self-Attention
We have a set of entities
Each of these has a vector representation
What do we want? We want to re-express the vector representations so that all entities know about all other entities
What is Self-Attention?
Big idea of the Transformer: Self-Attention
We have a particular entity v (a vector representation) and we have entity representations { v 1 , . . . , v T } for all the entities

blocks.text: Idea: Attention!
Self-Attention
For each key { k t } T t =1 you have a score score t = q ⊺ k t
For all the scores, you can apply the softmax to get alignment probabilities a = softmax ( score ) so a is a vector of probabilities
Then, we can represent q as ∑ T t =1 a t v t , a weighted sum of vectors

More on Self-Attention in NLP
At a high level, the mechanism above is called Self-Attention: you have a query q and you use its similarity with some keys { k t } T t =1 to get a expression for q in terms of { v t } T t =1
blocks.nearby_text_before: More on Self-Attention in NLP

blocks.text: Attention with Matrices
If you have T queries-key-values we can succinctly write the new representations is SoftMax
Attention (Q, K, V) = softmax (QK ⊺ √ d k) V
blocks.nearby_text_before: Attention with Matrices If you have T queries-key-values we can succinctly write the new representations is SoftMax
blocks.nearby_text_after: Notice: Across all tokens, you can get the new representations very efficiently! V ∈ R T × dv ; usually d v = d k In the NLP example, we went from x → q , k , v via W Q , K , V and then applied Attention; Q = XW Q , K = XW K , V = XW V
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula computes attention outputs from queries, keys, and values. The similarity scores QK^\top are scaled by \sqrt{d_k}, normalized with softmax, and then used to weight V.

blocks.text: Notice: Across all tokens, you can get the new representations very efficiently!

blocks.visual_description: Scaled dot-product attention is shown as a sequence of operations on Q, K, and V: a matrix multiplication between Q and K is scaled, optionally masked, passed through SoftMax, and then used in a final matrix multiplication with V. The graphic expresses the computational pipeline behind attention and the role of masking in the standard attention mechanism.
blocks.figure_focus: Scaled dot-product attention computation pipeline

blocks.visual_description: The figure shows a multi-head attention block where query, key, and value inputs are projected through linear layers into parallel scaled dot-product attention heads. The head outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear layer to produce the multi-head attention result.

blocks.text: Review of Attention
Starting with initial embeddings x t , this mechanism allows us
Get different representations of q t , k t and v t
Using the association between q t and k s , get a new representation of q t that's aware of all the other k s
Since we start with x t , this is like a contextual representation of x t in terms of all other x s , but in another space
Multi-Head Attention: Why do we have 1 set of matrices W Q , K , V ; why not have h of them and then concatenate the results?
blocks.nearby_text_before: Get different representations of q t, k t and v t Using the association between q t and k s, get a new representation of q t that's aware of all the other k s Since we start with x t, this is like a contextual representation of x t in terms of all other x s, but in another space
blocks.nearby_text_after: So, for each x t we get a new representation of total length d model → hd v We map back to dimension d model via W O Typically, d v = d k and we can pick h, d k so that d model = hd k

blocks.text: Back to the Transformer
The original Transformer was an Encoder-Decoder model
The main use case was Machine Translation, where we have a source sentence and want to translate to a target sentence Vectors
Encoder is no longer LSTM, it's a model based on Self-Attention
Word Vectors are not used, instead WordPiece tokenization is used (see later)

blocks.nearby_text_before: The original Transformer was an Encoder-Decoder model Word Vectors are not used, instead WordPiece tokenization is used (see later)
blocks.visual_description: An encoder receives word vectors and passes the encoded representation to a decoder, with the decoder producing the translation output. The figure emphasizes the original Transformer as an encoder-decoder architecture for translation, while noting that word vectors are used as the input representation here.
blocks.figure_focus: Transformer encoder-decoder translation architecture

blocks.text: Transformer Encoder: Position Embeddings
At a high level, we want to mix the representations x via Self-Attention so that a position x t is aware of the ones around it
This way, we can build a representation that's context-aware
If you look at Attention above, you'll notice that it is position invariant! We need to add in positional information
'a b c d' and 'd c a b' give the same head representation
To disallow this, we add positional embeddings so that we use x t + p t where p t tells us we are at the start, middle, etc of the sentence
blocks.nearby_text_before: This way, we can build a representation that's context-aware If you look at Attention above, you'll notice that it is position invariant! We need to add in positional information 'a b c d' and 'd c a b' give the same head representation
blocks.nearby_text_after: To help with gradients, we add residual connections and layer norm (LN) After each Self-Attention application, we pass each embedding through a Feed-Forward Neural Network with a ReLU activation that projects each token's embedding up to 4 ∗ d model dimension and then back down to d model space We repeat this process several times until we get a good enough representation

blocks.text: Transformer Decoder
Once we have the representation of the Encoder's input, we use the Decoder to generate new data
This is similar, but we can't look ahead
To get a new representation of y t via Self-Attention, we have to make sure that we always mix y t with only other y s that have s ≤ t
blocks.nearby_text_before: Transformer Decoder Once we have the representation of the Encoder's input, we use the Decoder to generate new data This is similar, but we can't look ahead
blocks.nearby_text_after: This is called Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention Practically, when we have ∑ T s =1 a ts v s we want to make sure a ts = 0 if s > t This can easily be done by using a triangular mask over the score matrix so that score ts = -∞ if s > t
blocks.text_raw: To get a new representation of y t via Self-Attention, we have to make sure that we always mix y t with only other y s that have s ≤ t

blocks.text: This is called Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention
Practically, when we have ∑ T s =1 a ts v s we want to make sure a ts = 0 if s > t

blocks.visual_description: A comparison table summarizes four layer types—self-attention, recurrent, convolutional, and restricted self-attention—using three measures: per-layer complexity, number of sequential operations, and maximum path length. It states that full self-attention has quadratic complexity in sequence length, while recurrent and convolutional layers have different tradeoffs in sequential steps and path length; restricted self-attention reduces complexity to depend on the neighborhood size r.
