blocks.text: What is WordPiece?
The authors used WordPiece which is a tokenization that expressed words like 'immaculate' as 2 tokens 'imma' + '##culate'
'immaculate' → 'imma' + '##culate'
The tokes you choose have an impact on your model ...
What is wrong with just using words and characters?
Characters will almost surely cover all words (no '[UNK]')
But characters lose semantic meaning
Also, 4 Words could map into 20 characters so then you have to keep a long context and have models which are good at memory
On the other hand, words require huge vocabularies and also what do you do when you have a new word?
Instead of the most granular (characters) or the widest (words), find a better middle ground
What is WordPiece?
High level idea of WordPiece:
Step 1: Begin with a set of character tokens
Step 2: Break up all words into tokens and get scores for tokens being next to each other
Step 3: From the most popular tokens next to each other, make a new token which is the concatenation of the above
Step 4: Given the new token, get a count as in Step 2 by greedily breaking up words into tokens
Step 5: Repeat Step 2 - Step 4 until you get a token universe of the desired size or you can't go on
The transformer used 32000 tokens as its vocabulary V
WordPiece: Learning tokens
Assume the Corpus is a bunch of words with frequencies
Tokenize each at the most granular level
For example, 'abc' gets tokenized to 'a', '##b', '##c' since 'b', 'c' are inside of a word
The above leads to each token having counts equal to:
Given the above, the vocabulary is
For any pair of tokens, define the 'information' score as
score (t 1, t 2) = freq (t 1, t 2) freq (t 1) freq (t 2)
blocks.nearby_text_before: Given the above, the vocabulary is → ['b', 'h', 'p', '##g', '##n', '##s', '##u'] For any pair of tokens, define the 'information' score as
blocks.nearby_text_after: Find the pair with the largest score, and create a new token by merging the tokens t 1 and t 2 ; consider the new data Repeat until you can't merge anymore or the vocabulary size is large enough
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines a score for a token pair using its pair frequency divided by the product of the individual token frequencies. It is used to rank which pair should be merged next.

blocks.text: Find the pair with the largest score, and create a new token by merging the tokens t 1 and t 2 ; consider the new data
Repeat until you can't merge anymore or the vocabulary size is large enough
Word-Piece: Decoding
With WordPiece, a word gets tokenized from left to right greedily; we try and use the largest token in the vocabulary as we go from left to right
If we get to a place where a subword is not in the vocabulary, we classify the whole word as '[UNK]'
For example
'bugs'
'hug'
'bum'
→ ['UNK'], since '##um' is not in the vocabulary
Outline
Before the Transformer
The Transformer
GPT1
The Transformer was a Encoder-Decoder Model, and after it came out it got a lot of buzz
How do you make it faster?
How do you apply it to other tasks?
What if you try and make it bigger?
What if you use just the Decoder? (GPT)
What if you use just the Encoder? And, how? (BERT)
Let's look at GPT1 which addresses some of the above
GPT1: A big model for its time
Transformer with 12 layers
768-dimensional representation of the token vectors; feed forward layers were 3072 dimensional
Byte-Pair encoding tokenization with 40,000 merges
Trained on book corpus: over 700 unique books
Contains long spans of contiguous text, good for learning long range dependencies
The acronym 'GPT' was never in the paper, but seems like it means 'Generative Pretrained Transformer'
GPT model sizes over time
GPT 1: 120 Million
GPT 2: 1.5 Billion
GPT 3: 175 Billion
ChatGpt: 175 Billion
GPT 4: XYZ Trilion ?
GPT1 ideas
The idea was to train GPT1 on some large corpus as a language model and then 'fine tune it for some task'
This idea we saw in ULM-Fit, COVE, but it dates back to 2015 and the paper below which pretrains an LSTM and then uses it to Encode a piece of text which is then fed to a classifier
'he second approach is to use a sequence autoencoder, which reads the input sc quence into a vector and predicts the input sequence again. These two algorithms
can be used as a "pretraining" step for a later supervised sequence learning algo- rithm. In other words, the parameters obtained from the unsupervised step can be
used as a starting point for other supervised training models. In our experiments ve find that long short term memory recurrent networks after being pretrained
with the two approaches are more stable and generalize better.
ing, we are able to train long short term recurrent networks up to a few hundre timesteps, thereby achieving strong performance in many text classification tasks.
GPT1 ideas
Open AI researches trained the GPT1 model on a large corpus and fine-tuned it to 4 main tasks Layer Norm
Entailment: A: 'I walk home' B: 'My dog is happy to see me arrive'; predict if P ( B follows A ) or not
transformations for fine-tuning on different tasks.
We convert all structured inputs into token sequences to be processed by our pre-trained model, followed by a linear +softmax layer.

Identify the most frequent pair and add the rule ( t 1 , t 2 ) → t 1 t 2

blocks.text: BERT: An Encoder Transformer
BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
BERT was a model that uses the Transformer Encoder architecture in novel way
An Encoder essentially takes in some input and outputs the same input in a refined way
Imagine we insert a set of tokens and run them through a Transformer Encoder
Given bidirectional Encoder's context, it is unclear what the objective function would be; we know everything to the left and right
For ELMo, notice that the model is weakly bidirectional

blocks.text: BERT Input
my dog is cute (SEP) he likes play #ring (SEP)
Input of BERT is a pair of 2 segments ( A , B )
Tokenize A and B and insert 3 special tokens: [CLS] before A , and 2 [SEP] tokens, one between A and B and another after B Embeddings
Add in positional embeddings which contain information related to where a token is
Use segment embeddings to denote if we are looking at a token in the first or second sentence
One question we can ask (NSP): Does B follow A ?
NSP = Next Sentence Prediction

blocks.text: Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
In the 1950s, one Language Modeling NLP objective was the Cloze task: Fill in the missing blank C
'I am to the store but my car is acting up.'
Probably you can guess that 'driving' should go in the above
Essentially, BERT took MLM and NSP as the objective
MLM: Mask some words. What are the missing words?
Next Sentence Prediction: Pick two segments as A and B . Does B follow A ?

blocks.nearby_text_before: Probably you can guess that 'driving' should go in the above Essentially, BERT took MLM and NSP as the objective MLM: Mask some words. What are the missing words?
blocks.visual_description: BERT pre-training is shown with two objectives: next sentence prediction and masked language modeling. An input sentence pair contains masked tokens, and the model predicts the missing words from the surrounding context while also using the [CLS] representation for the NSP task.
blocks.figure_focus: BERT pre-training with MLM and NSP

blocks.text: Objective Details
An input to BERT might look like below
x original = '[CLS] my dog is nice [SEP] he likes play ##ing [SEP]'
x = '[CLS] my dog is [MASK] [SEP] he [MASK] play ##ing [SEP]'
y = (1, 'nice', 'likes')
You only predict masked tokens and the 1/0 NSP task on top of [CLS]
How much do you mask?
Authors proposed masking 15% of all tokens
Of these, 80% become mask in the training data
10% remain as is but have a loss term
10% get a random word inserted on the x side and the loss needs to recover the right word
The authors figured that doing the above would force the model to be on its toes: it needs to get good representations for everything not just tokens which have '[MLM]'
At test time, '[MLM]' is not used, so the above tries to make training ∼ test
What can MLM teach a model?
What can we learn from the below?
'Columbia University is located in , New York.'
'New York'
Learning Trivia!
'I started car and drove to the restaurant'
'my'
'the'
'his'
Syntactic categories!
'I walked across the street, and checked the traffic over shoulder.'
'my'
This seems to imply some sort of coreference!
Notes on BERT
A better example for data might be as below
R ( word ) means the word got replaced with a random word
I ( word ) means the word is the same
x original = '[CLS] my dog is an amazing animal [SEP] he likes play ##ing [SEP]'
x = '[CLS] my I(dog) is an [MASK] R(car) [SEP] he [MASK] play ##ing [SEP]'
y = (1, 'dog', 'amazing', 'animal', 'likes')

Fine Tuning BERT: General Ideas
Why does fine tuning work?
Solving a problem directly might be hard - we need good inputs
Via fine tuning, we are giving the model aimed at solving (main) Task 1 a head start Classifier (Feed-forward
Neural models are highly non-convex, so initialization matters
We learn general features from that Task 2 (LM), and then either use them (static) or fine tune them to the specific task
Typically, the (main) Task 1 is framed as a combination of Task 2 ' s architecture and a Task 1 specific 'head'

blocks.text: Results on SWAG
SWAG is a dataset where you are given a sentence and asked to choose among 4 plausible next sentences
For each element in SWAG, select the source sentence and 1 of each of the 4 other sentences to get 4 inputs to BERT
In each case, grab the [CLS] token embedding C and and score one sentence continuing another via a score until you get scores s 1 , s 2 , s 3 and s 4
Put the scores through a softmax and back propagate using the true answer is the label

Suppose we have a large model, which performs really well
What are some problems?
Maybe it is very hard to train this model
Maybe the inference speed is prohibitively large
Maybe you need the model on a phone ('Edge Device'), and you don't have memory
Knowledge Distillation offers one way to addresses this issue: you use the larger (teacher) model to help a smaller (student) model learn
Let's look at the case where the task is Classification with C classes

blocks.formula_explanation: This is a weighted sum of two cross-entropy terms. The first term trains the student on true labels, and the second term matches the student’s softened output to the teacher’s softened output, scaled by \(T^2\).

Objective 2: Teach student to behave like Teacher, who is good at predicting y but is too large
Knowledge Distillation
What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
You want the student to still learn things from the teacher; here, 'learns' means same probabilities per target
blocks.nearby_text_before: Knowledge Distillation What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
blocks.nearby_text_after: You want the student answers to be similar to the teacher answers Case 1: If the teacher is too sure ( p (1) j is high for one particular j ), T →∞ smooths outs the distribution so p ( T ) becomes uniform; this adds a bit of hedging Case 2: If you want to just learn from the teacher directly, you can use T ∼ 0 and in the limit you use y t j = 1 if j = argmax ( p i ) otherwise y t j = 0

blocks.visual_description: Tables compare DistilBERT with BERT and ELMo on GLUE, task transfer benchmarks, and inference efficiency. DistilBERT retains most of BERT's accuracy while using fewer parameters and lower inference time, and the accompanying notes emphasize that student initialization from teacher weights matters most, while the MLM loss is least crucial.

BERT

Distil-BERT
Sentence BERT
Main Idea
Goal: How do we get better Sentence level embeddings from BERT?
Typically, one can do any of:
Use the [CLS] token embedding
Use the mean of the embeddings for all other tokens in the sentence
Is this the right thing to do?
What is wrong with doing this?
Remember: The above is if you use BERT directly; feed sentences through and getting a representation ... We expect similar sentences to have similar embeddings ...
BERT for sentence embeddings: GloVe is better

blocks.nearby_text_after: From the above, get the cosine similarity between A and B, which should be related to a gold label between 0 - 5 Get the rank correlation between the gold label and the cosine similarities: higher means model gives good quality embeddings It was found that using the CLS token embedding or mean pooling was weaker than using GloVe from 2014!
blocks.section_title: BERT for sentence embeddings: GloVe is better
blocks.visual_description: A comparison table reports sentence embedding performance across STS12, STS13, STS14, STS15, STS16, STSb, SICK-R, and average score for three models: average GloVe embeddings, average BERT embeddings, and the BERT CLS-vector. The GloVe embeddings row has the highest scores overall, while the BERT CLS-vector is consistently much weaker, supporting the claim that simple GloVe averaging outperforms these BERT pooling choices for sentence similarity.
blocks.figure_focus: Sentence embedding performance comparison: GloVe vs BERT

blocks.text: What they did:
For two sentences A and B embed them and get their embeddings (GLOVE/Word2Vec average, CLS token, average BERT embeddings, Etc)
From the above, get the cosine similarity between A and B, which should be related to a gold label between 0 - 5
Get the rank correlation between the gold label and the cosine similarities: higher means model gives good quality embeddings
It was found that using the CLS token embedding or mean pooling was weaker than using GloVe from 2014!
In fact, on many tasks averaged GloVe embeddings did much better!
CLS or BERT pooling don't look too well at all
Is there a better way?
Using BERT for sentence embeddings

blocks.section_title: Fast Retrieval utilizing the Bi Encoder
blocks.visual_description: A bi-encoder retrieval pipeline is shown where a query u is first encoded by BERT to obtain its embedding, then matched against a database of precomputed embeddings for many candidate v vectors using FAISS or kNN. The diagram emphasizes that candidate embeddings are stored ahead of time, so retrieval at inference avoids a forward pass over all candidates.

blocks.formula_explanation: The visible expression applies a softmax to a linear score built from the two sentence embeddings $u$ and $v$ together with their elementwise difference $|u-v|$. It represents a classifier over the pair of sentence embeddings.

blocks.formula_explanation: The visible formula is the squared norm of the quantity \(1-\cos(u,v)\). It measures how far the cosine similarity between \(u\) and \(v\) is from 1, with larger values meaning less similar embeddings.

blocks.text: Optimize the Siamese classical objective:
max (|| u a -u p || - || u a -u n || + ϵ, 0)
blocks.nearby_text_before: We can get this data from SNLI datasets, and back propagate gradients as needed to modify BERT so it gives better embeddings Add a regression objective via a similarity measure Optimize the Siamese classical objective
blocks.nearby_text_after: Here, u x is the embedding for some sentence x explicitly a is the anchor sentence and p is the positive sentence while n is the negative sentence The idea is we want the distance between p and a to be at least ϵ less than the distance between n and a
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula takes the positive part of the margin between two distances: the anchor–positive distance and the anchor–negative distance. It is zero when the negative example is already farther from the anchor by at least \(\epsilon\), and otherwise penalizes the violation.

blocks.text: Training and Inference with Sentence BERT
Training
Take BERT or ROBERTA to start and fine-tune on SNLI / MultiNLI data sentence pairs data where each pair is classified as contradiction, entailment, and neutral
Inference
Take sentences and run them through the fine-tuned model, cache embedding results
When you compare two sentences, evaluate the cosine similarity
If a sentence is new, run it through BERT

blocks.nearby_text_before: Take BERT or ROBERTA to start and fine-tune on SNLI / MultiNLI data sentence pairs data where each pair is classified as contradiction, entailment, and neutral Take sentences and run them through the fine-tuned model, cache embedding results
blocks.visual_description: Two sentence inputs are encoded by separate BERT towers with tied weights, then pooled into vectors u and v. One version combines u, v, and their absolute difference for a softmax classifier, while another computes cosine similarity between u and v to produce a score in the range -1 to 1.
blocks.figure_focus: SBERT inference and similarity scoring

blocks.text: Using BERT for sentence embeddings

blocks.visual_description: A table compares sentence embedding methods on computation speed and semantic textual similarity benchmarks. The speed table reports sentences per second on CPU and GPU for Avg. GloVe embeddings, InferSent, Universal Sentence Encoder, and SBERT variants, while the lower table reports Spearman rank correlation across STS12–STS16, STSb, and SICK-R with average scores.
blocks.figure_focus: Sentence embedding speed and STS performance comparison

blocks.text: Inference Quality: They did numerous studies and found that the output sentences significantly did better than BERT / ROBERTA and GLOVE across many tasks
Inference Speed: Still slower than GLOVE lookups, but smart batching allows for very fast GPU processing in the worst case
